License: CC BY 4.0
arXiv:2604.22394v2 [math.DG] 28 Apr 2026

Multiplicative Ehresmann connections for Lie groupoid fibrations

Matthijs Lau and Ioan Mărcu t , Dedicated to Rui Loja Fernandes on his 60th birthday
Abstract.

We introduce multiplicative Ehresmann connections on surjective submersions of Lie groupoids, extending both the classical notion of Ehresmann connections on fibre bundles and the more recent notion of multiplicative connections on Lie groupoid extensions. We investigate the existence of such connections, showing that, in general, they may fail to exist even for proper Lie groupoids. In contrast, positive results hold for Morita submersions, uniform Lie groupoid fibrations, locally trivial families of Lie groupoids, and proper families of Lie groupoids. Our main results concern completeness. For Lie groupoid fibrations, we prove that the completeness of a multiplicative connection is governed by the induced connection on the kernel bundle and, under connectivity assumptions, by the base connection. For families of source-proper Lie groupoids, we prove the equivalence between local triviality and the existence of complete multiplicative Ehresmann connections.

Matthijs Lau: DipMat, Università degli Studi di Salerno, via Giovanni Paolo II n∘ 123, 84084 Fisciano (SA), Italy, mlau@unisa.it
Ioan Mărcu
t
,
: Mathematisches Institut, Universität zu Köln, Weyertal 86-90, D-50931 Köln, Germany

  1. 1 Introduction
  2. 2 Preliminaries
  3. 3 Multiplicative Ehresmann connections
  4. 4 Existence
  5. 5 Completeness and the connection on the kernel
  6. 6 Families of Lie groupoids
  7. A Lie groupoid fibrations
  8. B 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoids
  9. References

1. Introduction

In many areas of differential geometry, fibrations play a fundamental role—most notably vector bundles, principal bundles, covering spaces, Riemannian submersions, and symplectic fibrations. These classes of fibrations come with corresponding notions of connections, defined by certain compatibility conditions with the internal geometric or algebraic structure of the fibration. In [20], the study of Lie groupoid extensions, i.e., surjective submersions of Lie groupoids covering the identity, and their corresponding notion of multiplicative Ehresmann connection was initiated. The authors introduced these connections in order to describe non-abelian differentiable gerbes over stacks and studied, among other things, the problems of existence and completeness. With the aim of producing local models in Poisson geometry [13], the study of multiplicative Ehresmann connections was taken up again in [12], where a strong existence result was obtained for proper groupoids and where the infinitesimal theory was developed. The cohomological obstruction theory at both the groupoid and algebroid levels was further developed in [14]. Multiplicative Ehresmann connections have found recent applications in the study of Yang–Mills theory on Lie groupoids [15] and in the construction of a Cartan model for the differentiable stack cohomology of Lie groupoids [19].

The purpose of the present paper is to extend the theory of multiplicative Ehresmann connections beyond extensions to Lie groupoid morphisms

π:(𝒢⇉M)→(ℋ⇉N)\pi\colon(\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M)\to(\mathcal{H}\rightrightarrows N)

which are surjective submersions, but whose base map π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon{M}\to N is not necessarily the identity. Also in this setting, multiplicative Ehresmann connections are defined by requiring their horizontal bundle to be a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid of T​𝒢⇉T​MT\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows TM

Hor⇉Hor0,\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0},

where Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is an induced connection for the base map π0\pi_{0}. These objects simultaneously generalise classical Ehresmann connections on surjective submersions, linear connections on vector bundles, multiplicative connections on Lie groupoid extensions, and, in particular, principal connections, via the gauge groupoid.

Section 3 is devoted to the basic properties of multiplicative Ehresmann connections. We show that they are characterized by the property that the horizontal paths form a subgroupoid of the path groupoid of 𝒢\mathcal{G} (Proposition 3.5). Finally, we briefly review the infinitesimal notions of multiplicative connections.

In Section 4 and at the beginning of Section 6, we turn to the existence problem for multiplicative Ehresmann connections. For extensions, properness of the total groupoid suffices to ensure existence [12]. The general case of Lie groupoid fibrations is much more restrictive. For example, the action morphism π:H⋉M→H\pi\colon H\ltimes M\to H corresponding to a non-trivial action of a connected group H↻MH\ {\rotatebox[origin={c}]{-90.0}{$\circlearrowright$}}\ M never admits a multiplicative connection (Corollary 4.3). We obtain positive results for the following classes of groupoid morphisms (reviewed in Section 2.2).

Theorem 1.1.

Multiplicative Ehresmann connections always exist for:

  1. (1)

    Morita fibrations (Proposition 4.4),

  2. (2)

    proper uniform Lie groupoid fibrations (Corollary 4.7),

  3. (3)

    locally trivial families of Lie groupoids (Proposition 6.3), and

  4. (4)

    proper families of Lie groupoids (Theorem 6.4).

The main results of the paper are about the completeness of multiplicative Ehresmann connections. Theorem 5.2, which generalizes [12] from extensions to fibrations, reduces the problem to the completeness of an induced connection on the kernel. Further, under the assumption of source-connectivity of the kernel, Corollary 5.4 and Theorem 5.6 reduce the problem to completeness of the base connection. The following statement summarises the results from Section 5.

Theorem 1.2.

Any multiplicative Ehresmann connection Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} on a Lie groupoid surjective submersion π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} induces a multiplicative Ehresmann connection Hor𝒦⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} on the kernel 𝒦:=ker⁡π→N\mathcal{K}:=\ker\pi\to N. The following implications hold regarding the completeness of these connections

where the dashed arrows assume the condition written underneath them.

Moreover, under the assumption of source-connectivity of ℋ\mathcal{H}, we show that being a fibration is also a necessary condition for completeness (Proposition 5.3).

The final Section 6 discusses a groupoid analogue of the equivalence between locally trivial fibrations and surjective submersions admitting complete Ehresmann connections—a result, whose correct proof was found only recently by Mathias del Hoyo [11]. By using the same techniques, we obtain a result for source-proper families of Lie groupoids in Theorem 6.7, which we state as an equivalence.

Theorem 1.3.

Let π:(𝒢⇉M)→N\pi\colon(\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M)\to N be a family of Lie groupoids, where 𝒢\mathcal{G} is a source-proper Lie groupoid. Then π\pi is a locally trivial family of Lie groupoids if and only if it admits a complete multiplicative Ehresmann connection.

Appendix A contains a discussion on the relation between notions of Lie groupoid fibration; in particular, Theorem A.1 gives a set of easily checkable conditions for a Lie groupoid surjective submersion to be a fibration. Appendix B is devoted to Proposition B.1, which gives a criterion for identifying 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoids.

Acknowledgements

We would like to thank Rui Loja Fernandes, Matias del Hoyo, Madeleine Jotz, and Žan Grad for useful discussions about multiplicative Ehresmann connections, and for their general interest in this project. We are also very grateful to João Mestre, who helped us understand the techniques of [6], which were used in the proof of Theorem 6.4, and to Luca Vitagliano, who found for us Example 3.4.

2. Preliminaries

2.1. Assumptions and conventions

Throughout the paper, all Lie groupoids are assumed to be Hausdorff, ensuring the uniqueness of solutions to the relevant flow problems. We denote a Lie groupoid by 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M, where 𝒢\mathcal{G} is the space of arrows, MM is the space of objects. The structure maps are written as 𝒔\boldsymbol{s} = source, 𝒕\boldsymbol{t} = target, 𝒎\boldsymbol{m} = multiplication, 𝒖\boldsymbol{u} = unit, and 𝒊\boldsymbol{i} = inversion. The source-fibres will be simply denoted by 𝒔−1​(x)\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(x), and we will avoid notations like 𝒢x\mathcal{G}_{x} or 𝒢​(x,−)\mathcal{G}(x,-); similarly for the target-fibres. We will identify the base with the unit section M≅𝒖​(M)⊂𝒢M\cong\boldsymbol{u}(M)\subset\mathcal{G}.

2.2. Classes of groupoid maps

We introduce the main classes of Lie groupoid morphisms used in this paper. The terminology follows [24]*Section 2.6 and [10]*Section 2.1.

Definition 2.1.

A Lie groupoid morphism π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} with base map π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N such that π\pi (and therefore also π0\pi_{0}) is a surjective submersion is called

  1. (1)

    a Lie groupoid surjective submersion;

  2. (2)

    a Lie groupoid fibration, if additionally the map

    (1) π×𝒔:𝒢→ℋ×π0𝒔M,g↦(π(g),𝒔(g))\pi\times\boldsymbol{s}\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H}{}_{\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{\pi_{0}}M,\qquad g\mapsto(\pi(g),\boldsymbol{s}(g))

    is a surjective submersion;

  3. (3)

    an action morphism, if the map from (1) is a diffeomorphism;

  4. (4)

    a family of Lie groupoids, if ℋ=N⇉N\mathcal{H}=N\rightrightarrows N is the unit groupoid;

  5. (5)

    a uniform Lie groupoid fibration, if the map

    (2) π∗:𝒢→π0∗​ℋ,g↦(𝒕​(g),π​(g),𝒔​(g))\pi^{*}\colon\mathcal{G}\to\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H},\qquad g\mapsto(\boldsymbol{t}(g),\pi(g),\boldsymbol{s}(g))

    is a surjective submersion, where π0∗​ℋ\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H} is the pullback groupoid:

    π0∗ℋ:=M×𝒕π0ℋ×π0𝒔M⇉M;\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H}:=M{}_{\pi_{0}}\times_{\boldsymbol{t}}\mathcal{H}{}_{\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{\pi_{0}}M\rightrightarrows M;
  6. (6)

    a Morita fibration (or inductor), if the map in (2) is an isomorphism;

  7. (7)

    a Lie groupoid extension, if the base map is the identity map, i.e. π0=idM\pi_{0}=\mathrm{id}_{M}.

 ▽\triangledown

In Appendix A we discuss the connections between these notions. In particular, we have the following strict implications:

The kernel ker⁡π:=π−1​(𝒖​(N))⇉M\ker\pi:=\pi^{-1}(\boldsymbol{u}(N))\rightrightarrows M of a Lie groupoid surjective submersion π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} is a subgroupoid of 𝒢\mathcal{G}. Furthermore, the restriction π¯:ker⁡π→N\underline{\pi}\colon\ker\pi\to N endows ker⁡π\ker\pi with the structure of a family of Lie groupoids parametrised by NN, the base of ℋ\mathcal{H}. Note that, beyond the case of extensions, the information of the morphism is not contained exclusively in the kernel. This is discussed in detail in [24], from where we borrow the following example.

Example 2.2.

Consider a principal bundle G↻M→NG\ {\rotatebox[origin={c}]{-90.0}{$\circlearrowright$}}\ M\to N. The groupoid map

π:M×M→(M×M)/G,\pi\colon M\times M\to(M\times M)/G,

from the pair groupoid to the gauge groupoid is a uniform Lie groupoid fibration whose kernel is just the identity subgroupoid.  △\vartriangle

Compared to general Lie groupoid surjective submersions, the situation for Lie groupoid fibrations is considerably better, as the data of the morphism is encoded by a so-called normal subgroupoid system, see again [24]*Section 2.4. This is the deeper reason why we can reduce the completeness problem for multiplicative connections to the kernel (see Theorem 5.2).

2.3. 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids

In order to define multiplicative connections, we quickly recall the basics of 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids (for a general reference, see [24] and [3]).

Definition 2.3.

A 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid is a tuple (Γ,E/⁣/𝒢,M)(\Gamma,E\mathbin{\mkern-2.0mu/\mkern-6.0mu/\mkern-2.0mu}\mathcal{G},M) such that:

  • •

    Γ⇉E\Gamma\rightrightarrows E and 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M are Lie groupoids;

  • •

    q~:Γ→𝒢\widetilde{q}\colon\Gamma\to\mathcal{G} and q:E→Mq\colon E\to M are vector bundles;

  • •

    q~\widetilde{q} is a Lie–groupoid morphism over qq, and the addition map

    +:Γ×q~q~Γ⟶Γ+\colon\Gamma{}_{\widetilde{q}}\times_{\widetilde{q}}\Gamma\longrightarrow\Gamma

    is a Lie–groupoid morphism covering the addition map

    +:E×qqE⟶E.+\colon E{}_{q}\times_{q}E\longrightarrow E.

 ▽\triangledown

A 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid (Γ,E/⁣/𝒢,M)(\Gamma,E\mathbin{\mkern-2.0mu/\mkern-6.0mu/\mkern-2.0mu}\mathcal{G},M) will be represented by the diagram:

We call the Lie groupoid Γ⇉E\Gamma\rightrightarrows E the total groupoid, whose structure maps we decorate with a tilde, and the projection q~:Γ→𝒢\widetilde{q}\colon\Gamma\to\mathcal{G} is the total vector bundle. The groupoid 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M is the base groupoid, and E→ME\to M is the side bundle. When no ambiguity may occur, we refer to a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid simply by its total space Γ\Gamma; when it is useful to emphasise the base, we say that Γ\Gamma is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid over 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M.

A 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid morphism from (Γ,E/⁣/𝒢,M)(\Gamma,E\mathbin{\mkern-2.0mu/\mkern-6.0mu/\mkern-2.0mu}\mathcal{G},M) to (Ω,F/⁣/ℋ,N)(\Omega,F\mathbin{\mkern-2.0mu/\mkern-6.0mu/\mkern-2.0mu}\mathcal{H},N) is a smooth map Φ:Γ→Ω\Phi\colon\Gamma\to\Omega which is simultaneously a vector bundle morphism over a Lie groupoid morphism ϕ:𝒢→ℋ\phi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} and a Lie groupoid morphism (Γ⇉E)→(Ω⇉F)(\Gamma\rightrightarrows E)\to(\Omega\rightrightarrows F).

A 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid of Γ\Gamma is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid Ω\Omega together with a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid morphism ι:Ω→Γ\iota\colon\Omega\to\Gamma whose underlying map of manifolds is an injective immersion and which identifies Ω\Omega with a vector subbundle of Γ\Gamma. Alternatively, these can simply be described as a simultaneous vector subbundle and subgroupoid, see Proposition B.1.

Consider a short exact sequence of 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids, all over the same groupoid 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M and covering the identity morphisms:

The exactness of the bottom row is implied by that of the top row, as the source maps are surjective and they admit the unit sections as a right inverse. Therefore, we typically only write the top row of a short exact sequence of 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids.

Note also that the image of the injective 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-map ι\iota is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid of Γ′\Gamma^{\prime}, and that Γ′′\Gamma^{\prime\prime} is determined up to isomorphism as Γ′/ι​(Γ)\Gamma^{\prime}/\iota(\Gamma). Similarly, if we are only given the surjective 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-map π\pi over id𝒢\mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{G}}, then it follows that ker⁡π\ker\pi is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid (see [21]*Corollary C.3 or Proposition B.1).

As for vector bundles (e.g. [28]*Theorem 27.20), split exact sequences of 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids are characterised as follows.

Lemma 2.4.

Consider a short exact sequence of 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids over 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M, say

Then, there is a 11-11 correspondence between the following objects:

  1. (1)

    𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid morphisms h:Γ′′→Γ′h\colon\Gamma^{\prime\prime}\to\Gamma^{\prime} such that π∘h=idΓ′′\pi\circ h=\mathrm{id}_{\Gamma^{\prime\prime}}.

  2. (2)

    𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid morphisms p:Γ′→Γp\colon\Gamma^{\prime}\to\Gamma such that p∘ι=idΓp\circ\iota=\mathrm{id}_{\Gamma}.

  3. (3)

    𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoids C⊂Γ′C\subset\Gamma^{\prime} complementing ι​(Γ)\iota(\Gamma) as a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid.

  4. (4)

    Splittings, i.e. 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid morphisms Φ:Γ′→Γ⊕Γ′′\Phi\colon\Gamma^{\prime}\to\Gamma\oplus\Gamma^{\prime\prime} fitting into the commutative diagram

    (Remark: it follows that Φ\Phi is an isomorphism.)

The correspondences are determined uniquely by

h∘π+ι∘p=idΓ′,C=ker⁡p=im​h,Φ=(p,π),Φ−1=ι⊕h.h\circ\pi+\iota\circ p=\mathrm{id}_{\Gamma^{\prime}},\qquad C=\ker p=\mathrm{im}h,\qquad\Phi=(p,\pi),\qquad\Phi^{-1}=\iota\oplus h.

Moreover, in (3), π|C:C→Γ′′\pi|_{C}\colon C\to\Gamma^{\prime\prime} is an isomorphism of 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids.

Proof.

The analogous statement for vector bundles is standard, and so it implies that the correspondences are uniquely determined. We need only show that these correspondences result in multiplicative maps and 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids.

(1)⇔\iff(4): Suppose h:Γ′′→Γ′h\colon\Gamma^{\prime\prime}\to\Gamma^{\prime} is a right splitting of the short exact sequence, such that π∘h=idΓ′′\pi\circ h=\mathrm{id}_{\Gamma^{\prime\prime}}. We define the isomorphism of vector bundles

ι⊕h:Γ⊕Γ′′→Γ′:(γ,γ′′)↦ι​(γ)+h​(γ′′).\iota\oplus h\colon\Gamma\oplus\Gamma^{\prime\prime}\to\Gamma^{\prime}\colon(\gamma,\gamma^{\prime\prime})\mapsto\iota(\gamma)+h(\gamma^{\prime\prime}).

As hh, ι\iota, and ++ are 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid maps, this will define a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid map as well. Defining Φ=(ι⊕h)−1\Phi=(\iota\oplus h)^{-1} gives a map satisfying condition (4).

Conversely, suppose that Φ:Γ′→Γ⊕Γ′′\Phi\colon\Gamma^{\prime}\to\Gamma\oplus\Gamma^{\prime\prime} is a splitting of the short exact sequence. We can then define a right inverse to π\pi as h:Γ′′→Γ′:γ′′↦ϕ−1​(incl2​(γ′′))h\colon\Gamma^{\prime\prime}\to\Gamma^{\prime}\colon\gamma^{\prime\prime}\mapsto\phi^{-1}(\mathrm{incl}_{2}(\gamma^{\prime\prime})).

(2)⇔\iff(4): Suppose that p:Γ′→Γp\colon\Gamma^{\prime}\to\Gamma is a left splitting, such that p∘ι=idΓp\circ\iota=\mathrm{id}_{\Gamma}. We define Φ\Phi as the map (p,π):Γ′→Γ⊕Γ′′:γ′↦(p​(γ′),π​(γ′))(p,\pi)\colon\Gamma^{\prime}\to\Gamma\oplus\Gamma^{\prime\prime}\colon\gamma^{\prime}\mapsto(p(\gamma^{\prime}),\pi(\gamma^{\prime})), which is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid map by the universal property of the direct sum. One easily verifies that this satisfies condition (4).

Conversely, given a splitting Φ:Γ′→Γ⊕Γ′′\Phi\colon\Gamma^{\prime}\to\Gamma\oplus\Gamma^{\prime\prime} we can define p:Γ′→Γ=pr1∘Φp\colon\Gamma^{\prime}\to\Gamma=\mathrm{pr}_{1}\circ\Phi.

(3)⇔\iff(4): One readily verifies this by chasing the diagram. ∎

2.4. Multiplicative distributions

The prototypical example of a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid is the tangent bundle of a Lie groupoid 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M:

Distributions and foliations in the multiplicative setting have been studied by several authors, e.g., in [8, 12, 16, 18, 17, 20, 27].

Definition 2.5.

A multiplicative distribution on a Lie groupoid 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M is a distribution 𝒟\mathcal{D} on 𝒢\mathcal{G} that is also a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid 𝒟⇉𝒟0\mathcal{D}\rightrightarrows\mathcal{D}_{0} of T​𝒢⇉T​MT\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows TM.

If 𝒟\mathcal{D} is involutive, we will call it a multiplicative foliation.  ▽\triangledown

Remark.

Multiplicative foliation 𝒟\mathcal{D} on 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M, the base 𝒟0⊂T​M\mathcal{D}_{0}\subset TM is also an involutive distribution [16]*Lemma 5.1; hence a foliation on MM. In fact, (𝒟,𝒟0/⁣/𝒢,M)(\mathcal{D},\mathcal{D}_{0}\mathbin{\mkern-2.0mu/\mkern-6.0mu/\mkern-2.0mu}\mathcal{G},M) forms a so-called ℒ​𝒜\mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}-groupoid, i.e. 𝒟\mathcal{D} and 𝒟0\mathcal{D}_{0} are Lie algebroids and all groupoid structure maps are Lie algebroid maps (see [23]). Multiplicative foliations therefore coincide with ℒ​𝒜\mathcal{L}\mathcal{A}-subgroupoid of T​𝒢⇉T​MT\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows TM.  △\vartriangle

Note that a multiplicative distribution gives rise to a short exact sequence of 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids, namely:

3. Multiplicative Ehresmann connections

We introduce the main notion studied in this paper. Consider a Lie groupoid surjective submersion π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} with base map π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N. Notice that ker⁡T​π\ker T\pi defines a multiplicative distribution on 𝒢\mathcal{G}; moreover, it gives rise to a short exact sequence of 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoids:

where T​ℋ×ℋ𝒢⇉T​N×NMT\mathcal{H}\times_{\mathcal{H}}\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows TN\times_{N}M is a fibre product of Lie groupoids.

Definition 3.1.

A multiplicative Ehresmann connection (MEC) is defined in either of the following ways, which are equivalent according to Lemma 2.4:

  • •

    Multiplicative horizontal lift: A 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid morphism

    hor:T​ℋ×ℋ𝒢→T​𝒢\displaystyle\operatorname{hor}\colon T\mathcal{H}\times_{\mathcal{H}}\mathcal{G}\to T\mathcal{G}\quad such that(T​π×pr)∘hor=idT​ℋ×ℋ𝒢.\displaystyle\textrm{such that}\quad(T\pi\times\mathrm{pr})\circ\operatorname{hor}=\mathrm{id}_{T\mathcal{H}\times_{\mathcal{H}}\mathcal{G}}.
  • •

    Multiplicative vertical projection: A 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid morphism

    		•
    vert:T​𝒢→ker⁡T​π\displaystyle\mathrm{vert}\colon T\mathcal{G}\to\ker T\pi\quad such thatvert∘ι=idker⁡T​π.\displaystyle\textrm{such that}\quad\mathrm{vert}\circ\iota=\mathrm{id}_{\ker T\pi}.
  • •

    Multiplicative Ehresmann connection: A 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid

    		•
    Hor⊂T​𝒢\displaystyle\operatorname{Hor}\subset T\mathcal{G}\quad such thatT​𝒢=Hor⊕ker⁡T​π.\displaystyle\textrm{such that}\quad T\mathcal{G}=\operatorname{Hor}\oplus\ker T\pi.

Then Hor0⊂T​M\operatorname{Hor}_{0}\subset TM, the base of the 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0}, defines a usual Ehresmann connection for the base map π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N. The base of the morphism hor\operatorname{hor} is the horizontal lift hor0:T​N×NM→T​M\operatorname{hor}_{0}\colon TN\times_{N}M\to TM with respect to Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}.  ▽\triangledown

Examples 3.2.

The following concepts fit into this framework.

  1. (1)

    If one considers a surjective submersion of manifolds q:M→Bq\colon M\to B as a surjective submersion of Lie groupoids between the unit groupoids, then MECs are just usual Ehresmann connections.

  2. (2)

    For a vector bundle π:E→M\pi\colon E\to M, viewed as a groupoid map from the bundle of abelian groups to the unit groupoid, MECs are the same as linear connections.

  3. (3)

    For Lie groupoid extensions π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H}, i.e. π0=idM\pi_{0}=\mathrm{id}_{M}, MECs were studied in detail in [20], and later in [12]. In particular, they extend connection forms on principal bundles via the gauge groupoid construction.

  4. (4)

    Multiplicative, affine connections on a Lie groupoid ℋ⇉N\mathcal{H}\rightrightarrows N have been introduced and studied in [26]. These are the same as affine connections ∇\nabla on ℋ\mathcal{H} which, when seen as an Ehresmann connection on the surjective Lie groupoid surjective submersion π:T​ℋ→ℋ\pi\colon T\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}, are multiplicative in our sense, see [26]*Proposition 3.3. The authors of [26] also developed cohomological obstruction theory for their existence, and show that these objects are very restrictive. In particular, their existence implies that ℋ\mathcal{H} is regular and that the connected components of its isotropy groups are abelian.

 △\vartriangle

For later use, let us remark that multiplicative Ehresmann connections lift infinitesimal groupoid automorphisms to infinitesimal groupoid automorphisms (for the basics on multiplicative vector fields on Lie groupoids, see [22]).

Proposition 3.3.

A multiplicative horizontal lift hor:T​ℋ×ℋ𝒢→T​𝒢\operatorname{hor}\colon T\mathcal{H}\times_{\mathcal{H}}\mathcal{G}\to T\mathcal{G} on π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} sends multiplicative vector fields to multiplicative vector fields

hor:𝔛mult​(ℋ)→𝔛mult​(𝒢).\operatorname{hor}\colon\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{mult}}(\mathcal{H})\to\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{mult}}(\mathcal{G}).
Proof.

A vector field XX on ℋ\mathcal{H} is multiplicative precisely when X:ℋ→T​ℋX\colon\mathcal{H}\to T\mathcal{H} is a groupoid morphism. If this is the case, then so is the pullback section, i.e. the map

X~:𝒢→T​ℋ×ℋ𝒢:g↦(Xπ​(g),g).\widetilde{X}\colon\mathcal{G}\to T\mathcal{H}\times_{\mathcal{H}}\mathcal{G}\colon g\mapsto(X_{\pi(g)},g).

The vector field hor⁡(X)\operatorname{hor}(X) is obtained by composing this map with the 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid morphism hor:T​ℋ×ℋ𝒢→T​𝒢\operatorname{hor}\colon T\mathcal{H}\times_{\mathcal{H}}\mathcal{G}\to T\mathcal{G}. Hence, the result. ∎

The above proposition does not have a converse and therefore does not fully characterise a multiplicative Ehresmann connection. The following example was constructed by Luca Vitagliano. It shows that, in general, the conclusion of Proposition 3.3 is not sufficient to ensure multiplicativity.

Example 3.4.

Consider the surjective submersion π:ℝ2→S1,(x,y)↦ei​x\pi\colon\mathbb{R}^{2}\to S^{1},\,(x,y)\mapsto e^{ix}, and remark that this is a Lie group(oid) homomorphism with respect to the standard structures. Any multiplicative vector field on S1S^{1} must be the zero vector field. Therefore, any connection hor\operatorname{hor} on π\pi will trivially induce a map

hor:𝔛mult​(S1)→𝔛mult​(ℝ2).\operatorname{hor}\colon\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{mult}}(S^{1})\to\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{mult}}(\mathbb{R}^{2}).

s As ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2} is an abelian Lie group, the left and right trivialization of T​ℝ2T\mathbb{R}^{2} coincide. Moreover, as a Lie group, it is isomorphic to the standard structure on ℝ4\mathbb{R}^{4} via the left/right trivialization. In this global chart, the vertical bundle is given by:

ker⁡T​π=span​{(x,y,a,b)∈ℝ4∣a=0}\ker T\pi=\mathrm{span}\{(x,y,a,b)\in\mathbb{R}^{4}\mid a=0\}

Next, consider a distribution, given in the global chart as:

Hor={(x,y,a,b)∈ℝ4∣b=x2​a}.\operatorname{Hor}=\{(x,y,a,b)\in\mathbb{R}^{4}\mid b=x^{2}a\}.

Fibrewise, meaning for fixed (x,y)∈ℝ2(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^{2}, it defines a linear complement and thus a connection. However, it is easy to see that Hor\operatorname{Hor} is not a subgroup of T​ℝ2T\mathbb{R}^{2} as for any (x,y,a,b)∈Hor(x,y,a,b)\in\operatorname{Hor}, we have that 2​(x,y,a,b)=(2​x,2​y,2​a,2​b)∉Hor2(x,y,a,b)=(2x,2y,2a,2b)\notin\operatorname{Hor}.  △\vartriangle

3.1. Lifting of paths

In this subsection, we interpret multiplicativity of a connection from a geometric perspective, using the lifting of paths. In particular, we obtain characterizations similar to [27]*Definition 2.1, and generalizing [20]*Proposition 4.3.

Fix a Lie groupoid surjective submersion π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} with an Ehresmann connection

T​𝒢=Hor⊕ker⁡T​π,T\mathcal{G}=\operatorname{Hor}\oplus\ker T\pi,

and denote the horizontal lift operator by hor:T​ℋ×ℋ𝒢→T​𝒢\operatorname{hor}\colon T\mathcal{H}\times_{\mathcal{H}}\mathcal{G}\to T\mathcal{G}. Given a curve γ:I→ℋ\gamma\colon I\to\mathcal{H}, where we will always denote I=[0,1]I=[0,1], with h:=γ​(0)h:=\gamma(0) and a point g∈π−1​(h)g\in\pi^{-1}(h), there exists a unique horizontal lift of γ\gamma through gg, namely a curve

τγ​(g):I0→M,t↦τγt​(g)\tau_{\gamma}(g)\colon I^{0}\to M,\qquad t\mapsto\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g)

solving the ordinary differential equation

{dd​t​τγt​(g)=hor⁡(τγt​(g),dd​t​γ​(t)),t∈I,τγ0​(g)=g,\begin{cases}\tfrac{d}{dt}\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g)=\operatorname{hor}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g),\tfrac{d}{dt}\gamma(t)),&t\in I,\\ \tau_{\gamma}^{0}(g)=g,\end{cases}

where 0∈I0⊂I0\in I^{0}\subset I is the maximal domain of the solution. The connection is complete precisely when I0=II^{0}=I, for all γ\gamma and gg as above.

The holonomy of γ\gamma or parallel transport along γ\gamma is the map

τγt:Ut⊂π−1(γ(0))→π−1(γ(t)),g↦τγt(g),\tau_{\gamma}^{t}\colon U^{t}\subset\pi^{-1}(\gamma(0))\to\pi^{-1}(\gamma(t)),\qquad g\mapsto\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g),

where UtU^{t} is the maximal domain on which it is defined.

Additionally, in the following description of multiplicativity in terms of holonomy, we use the notion of the so-called current groupoid structure on the space of smooth paths in a Lie groupoid 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M, studied in [1]:

C∞​(I,𝒢)⇉C∞​(I,M),C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})\rightrightarrows C^{\infty}(I,M),

where the structure maps are pointwise, i.e.

𝒔​(γ)​(t)=𝒔​(γ​(t)),𝒎​(γ,η)​(t)=𝒎​(γ​(t),η​(t)), 1γ​(t)=1γ​(t),γ−1​(t)=(γ​(t))−1.\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)(t)=\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma(t)),\ \ \boldsymbol{m}(\gamma,\eta)(t)=\boldsymbol{m}(\gamma(t),\eta(t)),\ \ 1_{\gamma}(t)=1_{\gamma(t)},\ \ \gamma^{-1}(t)=(\gamma(t))^{-1}.
Proposition 3.5.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a Lie groupoid surjective submersion covering π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N, and suppose Hor\operatorname{Hor} and Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} be connections for π\pi and π0\pi_{0}, respectively. The following are equivalent:

  1. (1)

    Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is a multiplicative Ehresmann connection.

  2. (2)

    The following hold:

    1. (a)

      For any δ∈C∞​(I,N)\delta\in C^{\infty}(I,N) and any x∈Mx\in M with π0​(x)=δ​(0)\pi_{0}(x)=\delta(0), if the Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}-lift τδt​(x)\tau^{t}_{\delta}(x) is defined, then

      𝒖​(τδt​(x))\displaystyle\boldsymbol{u}(\tau_{\delta}^{t}(x)) =τ𝒖​(δ)t​(𝒖​(x));\displaystyle=\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{u}(\delta)}(\boldsymbol{u}(x));
    2. (b)

      for any γ∈C∞​(I,ℋ)\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{H}) and any g∈𝒢g\in\mathcal{G} with π​(g)=γ​(0)\pi(g)=\gamma(0), if the Hor\operatorname{Hor}-lift τγt​(g)\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g) is defined, then

      		3.
      𝒔​(τγt​(g))=τ𝒔​(γ)t​(𝒔​(g))\displaystyle\boldsymbol{s}(\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g))=\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}(\boldsymbol{s}(g)) ,𝒕(τγt(g))=τ𝒕​(γ)t(𝒕(g)),\displaystyle,\quad\boldsymbol{t}(\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g))=\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma)}(\boldsymbol{t}(g)),
      𝒊​(τγt​(g))\displaystyle\boldsymbol{i}\big(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g)\big) =τ𝒊​(γ)t​(𝒊​(g));\displaystyle=\tau_{\boldsymbol{i}(\gamma)}^{t}(\boldsymbol{i}(g));
    3. (c)

      and for any η∈C∞​(I,ℋ)\eta\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{H}) with 𝒕​(η)=𝒔​(γ)\boldsymbol{t}(\eta)=\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma), and any k∈𝒢k\in\mathcal{G} with 𝒕​(k)=𝒔​(g)\boldsymbol{t}(k)=\boldsymbol{s}(g) and π​(k)=η​(0)\pi(k)=\eta(0), if the Hor\operatorname{Hor}-lift τηt​(h)\tau^{t}_{\eta}(h) is also defined, then

      		4.
      𝒎​(τγt​(g),τηt​(k))\displaystyle\boldsymbol{m}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g),\tau_{\eta}^{t}(k)) =τ𝒎​(γ,η)t​(𝒎​(g,k)).\displaystyle=\tau_{\boldsymbol{m}(\gamma,\eta)}^{t}(\boldsymbol{m}(g,k)).
Proof.

(1)⟹\implies(2): Suppose Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is a MEC.

(a): Let δ:I→N\delta\colon I\to N and x∈Mx\in M be as in the statement. Since T​𝒖:T​M→T​𝒢T\boldsymbol{u}\colon TM\to T\mathcal{G} is the unit section of T​𝒢T\mathcal{G}, we have that T​𝒖​(Hor0)=T​M∩HorT\boldsymbol{u}(\operatorname{Hor}_{0})=TM\cap\operatorname{Hor}. This yields

dd​t​𝒖​(τδt​(x))=T​𝒖​(dd​t​τδt​(x))∈Tτδt​(x)​M∩Hor𝒖​(τδt​(x)).\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{u}(\tau^{t}_{\delta}(x))=T\boldsymbol{u}\big(\tfrac{d}{dt}\tau^{t}_{\delta}(x)\big)\in T_{\tau^{t}_{\delta}(x)}M\cap\operatorname{Hor}_{\boldsymbol{u}(\tau^{t}_{\delta}(x))}.

So the path t↦𝒖∘τδt​(x)t\mapsto\boldsymbol{u}\circ\tau^{t}_{\delta}(x) is Hor\operatorname{Hor}-horizontal. It starts at 𝒖​(x)\boldsymbol{u}(x) and satisfies

π∘𝒖∘τδt​(x)=𝒖∘π0∘τδt​(x)=𝒖​(δ)​(t).\pi\circ\boldsymbol{u}\circ\tau^{t}_{\delta}(x)=\boldsymbol{u}\circ\pi_{0}\circ\tau^{t}_{\delta}(x)=\boldsymbol{u}(\delta)(t).

This implies the claimed equality.

(b): Let γ:I→ℋ\gamma\colon I\to\mathcal{H} and g∈𝒢g\in\mathcal{G} be as in the statement. The path t↦𝒔​(τγt​(g))∈Mt\mapsto\boldsymbol{s}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g))\in M satisfies:

dd​t​𝒔​(τγt​(g))=T​𝒔​(dd​t​τγt​(g))∈T​𝒔​(Horτγt​(g))=(Hor0)𝒔​(τγt​(g)),\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{s}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g))=T\boldsymbol{s}\big(\tfrac{d}{dt}\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g)\big)\in T\boldsymbol{s}(\operatorname{Hor}_{\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g)})=({\operatorname{Hor}_{0}})_{\boldsymbol{s}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g))},

so it is Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}-horizontal. Its base path is

π0∘𝒔​(τγt​(g))=𝒔​(π∘τγt​(g))=𝒔​(γ)​(t),\pi_{0}\circ\boldsymbol{s}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g))=\boldsymbol{s}(\pi\circ\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g))=\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)(t),

which implies that it is the Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}-horizontal lift of 𝒔∘γ\boldsymbol{s}\circ\gamma:

𝒔​(τγt​(g))=τ𝒔​(γ)t​(𝒔​(g)).\boldsymbol{s}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g))=\tau_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}^{t}(\boldsymbol{s}(g)).

The proof for the target is similar. Also, since T​𝒊​(Hor)=HorT\boldsymbol{i}(\operatorname{Hor})=\operatorname{Hor}, 𝒊​(τγt​(g))\boldsymbol{i}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g)) is a Hor\operatorname{Hor}-horizontal path covering 𝒊​(γ)\boldsymbol{i}(\gamma) and starting at 𝒊​(g)\boldsymbol{i}(g); hence the equality with the inverses.

(c): Let η\eta and hh be as in the statement. The assumptions and the previous result implies that the paths τγt​(g)\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g) and τηt​(k)\tau^{t}_{\eta}(k) are composable; indeed

𝒔​(τγt​(g))=τ𝒔∘γt​(𝒔​(g))=τ𝒕∘ηt​(𝒕​(k))=𝒕​(τηt​(g)).\boldsymbol{s}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g))=\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{s}\circ\gamma}(\boldsymbol{s}(g))=\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{t}\circ\eta}(\boldsymbol{t}(k))=\boldsymbol{t}(\tau_{\eta}^{t}(g)).

Next, T​𝒎​(Hor,Hor)=HorT\boldsymbol{m}(\operatorname{Hor},\operatorname{Hor})=\operatorname{Hor} implies that 𝒎​(τγt​(g),τηt​(k))\boldsymbol{m}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g),\tau_{\eta}^{t}(k)) is a horizontal path. Since

π​(𝒎​(τγt​(g),τηt​(k)))=𝒎​(π​(τγt​(g)),π​(τηt​(h)))=𝒎​(γ​(t),η​(t)),\pi(\boldsymbol{m}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g),\tau_{\eta}^{t}(k)))=\boldsymbol{m}(\pi(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g)),\pi(\tau_{\eta}^{t}(h)))=\boldsymbol{m}(\gamma(t),\eta(t)),

the last equality follows.

(2)⟹\implies(1): Suppose that the conditions in (2) hold. By Proposition B.1, we only need to show that Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is a subgroupoid of T​𝒢⇉T​MT\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows TM.

First, we show that T​𝒔​(Hor),T​𝒕​(Hor)⊂Hor0T\boldsymbol{s}(\operatorname{Hor}),T\boldsymbol{t}(\operatorname{Hor})\subset\operatorname{Hor}_{0} and T​𝒖​(Hor0)⊂HorT\boldsymbol{u}(\operatorname{Hor}_{0})\subset\operatorname{Hor}. Given v∈Horgv\in\operatorname{Hor}_{g}, let γ~∈C∞​(I,𝒢)\widetilde{\gamma}\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G}) be a Hor\operatorname{Hor}-horizontal curve with starting speed vv. Remark that this is the lift of γ=π∘γ~\gamma=\pi\circ\widetilde{\gamma} to gg. We can conclude that

T​𝒔​(v)=dd​t​𝒔​(γ~​(t))|t=0=dd​t​𝒔​(τγt​(g))|t=0=dd​t​τ𝒔​(γ)t​(𝒔​(g))|t=0∈Hor0.T\boldsymbol{s}(v)=\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{s}(\widetilde{\gamma}(t))\big|_{t=0}=\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{s}(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g))\big|_{t=0}=\tfrac{d}{dt}\tau_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}^{t}(\boldsymbol{s}(g))\big|_{t=0}\in\operatorname{Hor}_{0}.

Similarly, we can conclude that T​𝒕​(Hor)⊂Hor0T\boldsymbol{t}(\operatorname{Hor})\subset\operatorname{Hor}_{0}. Given v∈Hor0xv\in{\operatorname{Hor}_{0}}_{x}, take an integration γ~∈C∞​(I,M)Hor0\widetilde{\gamma}\in C^{\infty}(I,M)^{\operatorname{Hor}_{0}}, and set γ=π0∘γ~\gamma=\pi_{0}\circ\widetilde{\gamma}. Remark that γ~\widetilde{\gamma} is the horizontal lift of γ\gamma to γ~​(0)\widetilde{\gamma}(0). It follows that:

T​𝒖​(v)=dd​t​𝒖​(γ~​(t))|t=0=dd​t​𝒖​(τγt​(x))|t=0=dd​t​τ𝒖​(γ)t​(𝒖​(x))|t=0∈Hor.T\boldsymbol{u}(v)=\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{u}(\widetilde{\gamma}(t))\big|_{t=0}=\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{u}(\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(x))\big|_{t=0}=\tfrac{d}{dt}\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{u}(\gamma)}(\boldsymbol{u}(x))\big|_{t=0}\in\operatorname{Hor}.

Next, we show that T​𝒊​(Hor)⊂HorT\boldsymbol{i}(\operatorname{Hor})\subset\operatorname{Hor}. Let v∈Horgv\in\operatorname{Hor}_{g} and suppose that γ~∈C∞​(I,𝒢)\widetilde{\gamma}\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G}) is a Hor\operatorname{Hor}-horizontal curve with starting speed vv. Then this is the horizontal lift of γ=π∘γ~\gamma=\pi\circ\widetilde{\gamma} to gg such that the following holds:

T​𝒊​(v)=dd​t​𝒊​(γ~)|t=0=dd​t​𝒊​(τγt​(g))|t=0=dd​t​τ𝒊∘γt​(g−1)|t=0∈Hor.T\boldsymbol{i}(v)=\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{i}(\widetilde{\gamma})\big|_{t=0}=\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{i}(\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g))\big|_{t=0}=\tfrac{d}{dt}\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{i}\circ\gamma}(g^{-1})\big|_{t=0}\in\operatorname{Hor}.

Lastly, let (u,v)∈(Horg×Horh)∩T​𝒢(2)(u,v)\in(\operatorname{Hor}_{g}\times\operatorname{Hor}_{h})\cap T\mathcal{G}^{(2)}. Since (T​π​(u),T​π​(v))∈T​ℋ(2)(T\pi(u),T\pi(v))\in T\mathcal{H}^{(2)} there is (γ,η)∈C∞​(I,ℋ(2))(\gamma,\eta)\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{H}^{(2)}) with initial speed (T​π​(u),T​π​(v))(T\pi(u),T\pi(v)). Then

T​𝒎​(u,v)=dd​t​𝒎​(τγt​(g),τηt​(h))|t=0=dd​t​τ𝒎​(γ,η)t​(g​h)|t=0∈Hor.T\boldsymbol{m}(u,v)=\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{m}(\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g),\tau^{t}_{\eta}(h))\big|_{t=0}=\tfrac{d}{dt}\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{m}(\gamma,\eta)}(gh)\big|_{t=0}\in\operatorname{Hor}.

Therefore, (1) holds. ∎

Alternatively, we can obtain a description of multiplicative connections directly in terms of the current groupoids, which reduces to a more detailed description in the complete case. For this, we remark that the map

π×ev0:C∞(I,𝒢)→C∞(I,ℋ)×πev0𝒢,γ~↦(π∘γ~,γ~(0))\pi\times\mathrm{ev}_{0}\colon C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})\to C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{H}){}_{\mathrm{ev}_{0}}\times_{\pi}\mathcal{G},\qquad\widetilde{\gamma}\mapsto(\pi\circ\widetilde{\gamma},\widetilde{\gamma}(0))

is a groupoid morphism, where the groupoid on the right-hand side is the groupoid fibre product over ℋ\mathcal{H} and has base C∞(I,N)×π0ev0MC^{\infty}(I,N){}_{\mathrm{ev}_{0}}\times_{\pi_{0}}M, covering π0×ev0\pi_{0}\times\mathrm{ev}_{0}.

Proposition 3.6.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a Lie groupoid surjective submersion covering π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N, and suppose Hor\operatorname{Hor} and Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} be connections for π\pi and π0\pi_{0}, respectively. The following are equivalent:

  1. (1)

    Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is a multiplicative Ehresmann connection;

  2. (2)

    C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor⇉C∞​(I,M)Hor0C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}}\rightrightarrows C^{\infty}(I,M)^{\operatorname{Hor}_{0}} is a subgroupoid of the current groupoid C∞​(I,𝒢)C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G}).

In this case, the map π×ev0\pi\times\mathrm{ev}_{0} restricted to C∞​(I,𝒢)HorC^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}} is an injective groupoid morphism, and it is an isomorphism exactly when the connection is complete. The inverse is then given by the horizontal lift:

τ:C∞(I,ℋ)×πev0𝒢→∼C∞(I,𝒢)Hor,(γ,g)↦(t↦τγt(g)).\tau\colon C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{H}){}_{\mathrm{ev}_{0}}\times_{\pi}\mathcal{G}\xrightarrow{\raisebox{-1.68887pt}[0.0pt][0.0pt]{\smash{$\sim$}}}C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}},\qquad(\gamma,g)\mapsto\big(t\mapsto\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g)\big).
Proof.

(1)⟹\implies(2): Suppose that Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is a MEC. We claim that

(3) C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor∩C∞​(I,M)=C∞​(I,M)Hor0.C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}}\cap C^{\infty}(I,M)=C^{\infty}(I,M)^{\operatorname{Hor}_{0}}.

Indeed, let γ∈C∞​(I,𝒢)\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G}). It is in C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor∩C∞​(I,M)C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}}\cap C^{\infty}(I,M) if and only if dd​t​γ​(t)∈Hor\frac{d}{dt}\gamma(t)\in\operatorname{Hor} and γ​(t)∈M\gamma(t)\in M. Since Hor0=Hor∩T​M\operatorname{Hor}_{0}=\operatorname{Hor}\cap\,TM, this is equivalent to dd​t​γ​(t)∈Hor0\frac{d}{dt}\gamma(t)\in\operatorname{Hor}_{0}, thus to γ∈C∞​(I,M)Hor0\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,M)^{\operatorname{Hor}_{0}}.

It remains to check that C∞​(I,𝒢)HorC^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}} is closed under inversion and multiplication.

Given a path γ∈C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}}, we remark that:

dd​t​𝒊​(γ)​(t)=T​𝒊​(dd​t​γ​(t))∈T​𝒊​(Hor)⊂Hor,\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{i}(\gamma)(t)=T\boldsymbol{i}\big(\tfrac{d}{dt}\gamma(t)\big)\in T\boldsymbol{i}(\operatorname{Hor})\subset\operatorname{Hor},

so that 𝒊​(γ)∈C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor\boldsymbol{i}(\gamma)\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}}.

If additionally, η∈C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor\eta\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}} such that 𝒔​(γ)=𝒕​(η)\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)=\boldsymbol{t}(\eta). Then we find that:

dd​t​𝒎​(γ,η)​(t)=T​𝒎​(dd​t​γ​(t),dd​t​η​(t))∈T​𝒎​(Hor,Hor)⊂Hor.\tfrac{d}{dt}\boldsymbol{m}(\gamma,\eta)(t)=T\boldsymbol{m}\big(\tfrac{d}{dt}\gamma(t),\tfrac{d}{dt}\eta(t)\big)\in T\boldsymbol{m}(\operatorname{Hor},\operatorname{Hor})\subset\operatorname{Hor}.

Therefore, C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor⇉C∞​(I,M)Hor0C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}}\rightrightarrows C^{\infty}(I,M)^{\operatorname{Hor}_{0}} is a subgroupoid of the current groupoid.

(2)⟹\implies(1): The assumption implies Equation (3), and it is easy to see that this equality implies that Hor∩T​M=Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\cap\,TM=\operatorname{Hor}_{0}. Therefore, it suffices to show that Hor⊂T​𝒢\operatorname{Hor}\subset T\mathcal{G} is a set-theoretical subgroupoid; its base will be Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}, and, by Proposition B.1, it will be a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid. So Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} will be a MEC.

Let v∈Horv\in\operatorname{Hor}. Consider a path γ~∈C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor\widetilde{\gamma}\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}} with initial speed vv. By assumption, 𝒊∘γ~∈C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor\boldsymbol{i}\circ\widetilde{\gamma}\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}}. Therefore, the initial speed of this path lies in Hor\operatorname{Hor}. We obtain that T​𝒊​(Hor)⊂HorT\boldsymbol{i}(\operatorname{Hor})\subset\operatorname{Hor}.

Finally, take (u,v)∈(Horg×Hork)∩T​𝒢(2)(u,v)\in(\operatorname{Hor}_{g}\times\operatorname{Hor}_{k})\cap T\mathcal{G}^{(2)}. Consider a curve (γ,η):I→ℋ(2)(\gamma,\eta)\colon I\to\mathcal{H}^{(2)} with initial speed (T​π​(u),T​π​(v))(T\pi(u),T\pi(v)). Denote the lifts of γ\gamma and η\eta by γ~​(t):=τγt​(g)\widetilde{\gamma}(t):=\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g) and η~​(t):=τηt​(k)\widetilde{\eta}(t):=\tau_{\eta}^{t}(k) respectively. These paths are defined on [0,ε][0,\varepsilon], for some ε>0\varepsilon>0. After reparametrising all path via diffeomorphism I→∼[0,ε]I\xrightarrow{\raisebox{-1.59442pt}[0.0pt][0.0pt]{\smash{$\sim$}}}[0,\varepsilon] with initial speed 11, we may assume that γ~,η~∈C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor\widetilde{\gamma},\widetilde{\eta}\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}}. As we have seen above, 𝒔​(γ~)\boldsymbol{s}(\widetilde{\gamma}) and 𝒕​(η~)\boldsymbol{t}(\widetilde{\eta}) are Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}-horizontal paths, they cover the same path:

π0∘𝒔​(γ~)=𝒔​(π∘γ~)=𝒔​(γ)=𝒕​(η)=𝒕​(π∘η~)=π0∘𝒕​(η~),\pi_{0}\circ\boldsymbol{s}(\widetilde{\gamma})=\boldsymbol{s}(\pi\circ\widetilde{\gamma})=\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)=\boldsymbol{t}(\eta)=\boldsymbol{t}(\pi\circ\widetilde{\eta})=\pi_{0}\circ\boldsymbol{t}(\widetilde{\eta}),

and have the same initial value: 𝒔​(γ~)​(0)=𝒔​(g)=𝒕​(k)=𝒕​(η~)​(0)\boldsymbol{s}(\widetilde{\gamma})(0)=\boldsymbol{s}(g)=\boldsymbol{t}(k)=\boldsymbol{t}(\widetilde{\eta})(0). Hence 𝒔​(γ~)=𝒕​(η~)\boldsymbol{s}(\widetilde{\gamma})=\boldsymbol{t}(\widetilde{\eta}), so the paths γ~\widetilde{\gamma} and η~\widetilde{\eta} are composable. Per assumption (2), we have 𝒎​(γ~,η~)∈C∞​(I,𝒢)Hor\boldsymbol{m}(\widetilde{\gamma},\widetilde{\eta})\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})^{\operatorname{Hor}}. Calculating the initial speed, we obtain that T​𝒎​(v,w)∈HorT\boldsymbol{m}(v,w)\in\operatorname{Hor}.

Clearly, the maps π:C∞​(I,𝒢)→C∞​(I,ℋ)\pi\colon C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})\to C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{H}) and ev0:C∞​(I,𝒢)→𝒢\mathrm{ev}_{0}\colon C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{G})\to\mathcal{G} are groupoid morphisms, and clearly they induce a morphism to the pullback groupoid. Restricting this morphism to the subgroupoid of horizontal paths, we obtain the map from the statement. It is injective, because a horizontal path is uniquely determined by the base path and its initial value. The map is surjective, precisely when every path γ:I→ℋ\gamma\colon I\to\mathcal{H} has a lift γ~:I→𝒢\widetilde{\gamma}\colon I\to\mathcal{G} to any g∈π−1​(γ​(0))g\in\pi^{-1}(\gamma(0)). This is precisely equivalent to completeness of the connection, and clearly γ~=τγ​(g)\widetilde{\gamma}=\tau_{\gamma}(g). ∎

3.2. Infinitesimal pictures

In the following subsections, we briefly discuss the possible counterparts of the notion of multiplicative connection in the infinitesimal setting. We will not go into more detail in this paper.

3.2.1. Lie algebroid submersions

Given a Lie algebroid map Π:A→B\Pi\colon A\to B, which is a surjective submersion, the notion of an infinitesimal multiplicative Ehresmann connection (IMEC) is defined as a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-algebroid splitting T​A=ker⁡Π⊕EHorTA=\ker\Pi\oplus E_{\operatorname{Hor}}, where EHor⇒Hor0E_{\operatorname{Hor}}\Rightarrow\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subalgebroid of T​A⇒T​MTA\Rightarrow TM. Given a MEC Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} for a Lie groupoid surjective submersion π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H}, the Lie functor in the 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B} setting [3] yields an IMEC EHor=Lie​(Hor)E_{\operatorname{Hor}}=\mathrm{Lie}(\operatorname{Hor}) for the induced Lie algebroid surjective submersion Π:A→B\Pi\colon A\to B, where A=Lie​(𝒢)A=\mathrm{Lie}(\mathcal{G}) and B=Lie​(ℋ)B=\mathrm{Lie}(\mathcal{H}). Conversely, if 𝒢\mathcal{G} is source-simply connected, then any IMEC EHorE_{\operatorname{Hor}} for Π\Pi integrates to a MEC for π\pi. This result follows from [3]*Section 4.3. In the case of extensions, IMECs were studied in [12].

3.2.2. Multiplicative foliations

Instead of a Lie groupoid surjective submersion, we could consider a multiplicative foliation 𝒟\mathcal{D} on a Lie groupoid 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M, which replaces ker⁡T​π\ker T\pi. The corresponding notion of a MEC is simply a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid complement T​𝒢=𝒟⊕HorT\mathcal{G}=\mathcal{D}\oplus\operatorname{Hor}. For extensions, this point of view was very useful in [12] in showing that the existence of MECs is Morita invariant.

As shown in [18], a multiplicative (simple) foliation 𝒟⊂T​𝒢\mathcal{D}\subset T\mathcal{G} does not necessarily induce a (Lie) groupoid structure on 𝒢/𝒟\mathcal{G}/\mathcal{D}. Indeed assume that 𝒟\mathcal{D} and 𝒟0\mathcal{D}_{0} are both simple, so that quotient maps π:𝒢→𝒢/𝒟\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{G}/\mathcal{D} and π0:M→M/𝒟0\pi_{0}\colon M\to M/\mathcal{D}_{0} are surjective submersions. While the source, target, unit, and inverse map are well-defined on the quotients, the multiplication may fail to descend. Under the additional assumptions:

  1. (1)

    the map

    𝒢→M×𝒔¯π0𝒢/𝒟,g↦(𝒔(g),π(g))\mathcal{G}\to M{}_{\pi_{0}}\times_{\overline{\boldsymbol{s}}}\mathcal{G}/\mathcal{D},\qquad g\mapsto(\boldsymbol{s}(g),\pi(g))

    is surjective, and

  2. (2)

    if (g,h)∈𝒢(2)(g,h)\in\mathcal{G}^{(2)} and h∼1𝒔​(h)h\sim 1_{\boldsymbol{s}(h)} in 𝒢/𝒟\mathcal{G}/\mathcal{D} then h​g∼ghg\sim g in 𝒢/𝒟\mathcal{G}/\mathcal{D},

it was shown in [18] that 𝒢/𝒟⇉M/𝒟0\mathcal{G}/\mathcal{D}\rightrightarrows M/\mathcal{D}_{0} inherits a groupoid structure. In this case, π\pi is a Lie groupoid fibration (see Theorem A.1).

3.2.3. Infinitesimal ideal systems

To combine the settings discussed above, consider a Lie algebroid A⇒MA\Rightarrow M together with infinitesimally multiplicative foliation (IMF), i.e. an involutive subbundle E𝒟⊂T​AE_{\mathcal{D}}\subset TA which is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subalgebroid E𝒟⇒𝒟0E_{\mathcal{D}}\Rightarrow\mathcal{D}_{0}. These objects were studied in [17], where they are also described in terms of the more explicit data of an infinitesimal ideal system. Infinitesimally multiplicative foliations generalise the previous two settings:

  • •

    if Π:A→B\Pi\colon A\to B is a Lie algebroid submersion, then E𝒟:=ker⁡T​ΠE_{\mathcal{D}}:=\ker T\Pi is an IMF;

  • •

    if A=Lie​(𝒢)A=\mathrm{Lie}(\mathcal{G}), and 𝒟⊂T​𝒢\mathcal{D}\subset T\mathcal{G} is a multiplicative foliation, then E𝒟:=Lie​(𝒟)E_{\mathcal{D}}:=\mathrm{Lie}(\mathcal{D}) is an IMF. Moreover, if 𝒢\mathcal{G} is source-simply connected, then any IMF arises from a unique multiplicative foliation on 𝒢\mathcal{G} [17]*Theorem 4.8.

An IMEC EHorE_{\operatorname{Hor}} in this setting is a vector bundle complement T​A=E𝒟⊕EHorTA=E_{\mathcal{D}}\oplus E_{\operatorname{Hor}} which is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subalgebroid EHor⇒Hor0E_{\operatorname{Hor}}\Rightarrow\operatorname{Hor}_{0}.

Remark.

At the infinitesimal level, there also arise notions of Lie algebroid fibrations and uniform Lie algebroid fibrations, see [24]*Section 4.4. In conjunction with Appendix A, we notice, however, that at the infinitesimal level, both a Lie groupoid surjective submersion and Lie groupoid fibration define a Lie algebroid fibration. Somewhat analogously, uniform Lie groupoid fibrations descend to uniform Lie algebroid fibrations.  △\vartriangle

4. Existence

In this section, we discuss the problem of the existence of multiplicative Ehresmann connections in various contexts. Surjective submersions admit Ehresmann connections simply because any vector subbundle admits a complement. However, in the multiplicative setting, the existence problem is much more subtle, as 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoids do not always admit multiplicative complements.

4.1. Extensions

Consider the special case of an extension, i.e. a Lie groupoid surjective submersion π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} covering π0=idM:M→M\pi_{0}=\mathrm{id}_{M}\colon M\to M. In this case, the existence problem was well-researched already in [20], where a cohomological theory was developed, and an obstruction class was identified. In [12] and [14] the problem was studied in the more general framework of bundles of ideals on the 𝒢\mathcal{G}, which correspond to the notion of multiplicative foliations 𝒟⊂T​𝒢\mathcal{D}\subset T\mathcal{G} from Section 3.2.2 with trivial base 𝒟0=0M\mathcal{D}_{0}=0_{M}, and also in the setting of infinitesimal bundles of ideals in Lie algebroids, which correspond to Section 3.2.3 for 𝒟0=0M\mathcal{D}_{0}=0_{M}. In particular, [14] introduces a cohomological obstruction theory, both for the global level (Section 5.1.1), as well as for the infinitesimal level (Section 5.1.2). Existence was proven in [12]*Theorem 4.2 a bundle of ideals on a proper Lie groupoid; we recall the result in the particular case of extensions.

Theorem 4.1 ([12]).

Any Lie groupoid extension π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H}, defined on a proper Lie groupoid 𝒢\mathcal{G}, admits a multiplicative Ehresmann connection.

This result does not extend directly to general Lie groupoid fibrations. A class of counterexamples is presented in the next subsection.

4.2. Action morphisms

We discuss the existence of MECs for action morphisms. This case will provide examples showing that properness is not a sufficient condition for the existence of MECs.

Consider a Lie groupoid ℋ⇉N\mathcal{H}\rightrightarrows N with an action a:ℋ×π0𝒔M→Ma:\mathcal{H}{}_{\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{\pi_{0}}M\to M on a surjective submersion π0:M→N\pi_{0}:M\to N. This yields the action groupoid ℋ⋉M:=ℋ×π0𝒔M⇉M\mathcal{H}\ltimes M:=\mathcal{H}{}_{\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{\pi_{0}}M\rightrightarrows M, and the action morphism

(4) π:=pr1:ℋ⋉M→ℋ.\pi:=\mathrm{pr}_{1}:\mathcal{H}\ltimes M\to\mathcal{H}.

Up to isomorphism, any action morphism is of this type.

Proposition 4.2.

Any MEC Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} for (4) is determined by its base connection via the equality

(5) Hor=Tℋ×T​π0T​𝒔Hor0⇉Hor0.\operatorname{Hor}=T\mathcal{H}{}_{T\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{T\pi_{0}}\operatorname{Hor}_{0}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0}.

Conversely, given an Ehresmann connection Hor0⊂T​M\operatorname{Hor}_{0}\subset TM for π0\pi_{0}, the above formula defines a MEC precisely when it is T​ℋT\mathcal{H}-invariant:

Ta(Tℋ×T​π0T​𝒔Hor0)⊂Hor0.Ta(T\mathcal{H}{}_{T\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{T\pi_{0}}\operatorname{Hor}_{0})\subset\operatorname{Hor}_{0}.

In particular, such a connection satisfies ρ(Lie(ℋ)×NM))⊂Hor0\rho(\mathrm{Lie}(\mathcal{H})\times_{N}M))\subset\operatorname{Hor}_{0}, where ρ\rho is denotes the infinitesimal action of Lie​(ℋ)\mathrm{Lie}(\mathcal{H}) on π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N.

Proof.

The tangent of the action groupoid is also a tangent groupoid:

T​(ℋ⋉M)=T​ℋ⋉T​M.T(\mathcal{H}\ltimes M)=T\mathcal{H}\ltimes TM.

Therefore, if Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is a MEC, then Hor⊂Tℋ×T​π0T​𝒔Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\subset T\mathcal{H}{}_{T\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{T\pi_{0}}\operatorname{Hor}_{0}. Since the two bundles have the same rank, (5) follows.

Conversely, (5) defines a subgroupoid precisely when Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is T​ℋT\mathcal{H}-invariant.

The last inclusion follows from T​ℋT\mathcal{H}-invariance

ρ(Lie(ℋ)×NM)=Ta(Lie(ℋ)×T​π0T​𝒔0M)⊂Hor0.∎\rho(\mathrm{Lie}(\mathcal{H})\times_{N}M)=Ta(\mathrm{Lie}(\mathcal{H}){}_{T\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{T\pi_{0}}0_{M})\subset\operatorname{Hor}_{0}.\qed

For the particular case of groups, this implies the following.

Corollary 4.3.

Let HH be a connected Lie group acting on a manifold MM. The Lie groupoid fibration

π:=pr1:H⋉M→H,\pi:=\mathrm{pr}_{1}\colon H\ltimes M\to H,

admits a multiplicative Ehresmann connection only if the action is trivial.

Proof.

Note that Hor0=0M\operatorname{Hor}_{0}=0_{M}, because it is a connection on π0:M→{∗}\pi_{0}\colon M\to\{*\}. The proposition implies that the infinitesimal action of Lie​(H)\mathrm{Lie}(H) on MM is trivial. Since HH is connected, so is the action of HH on MM. ∎

We conclude that any non-trivial action of a compact, connected Lie group on a compact manifold yields an example of a compact Lie groupoid fibration that does not admit a MEC.

4.3. Morita fibrations

For this class of maps, we have a general existence result.

Proposition 4.4.

Any Morita fibration π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} admits a MEC. More precisely, for any Ehresmann connection Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} for the base map π0:M→N\pi_{0}:M\to N, there is a unique MEC Hor\operatorname{Hor} for π\pi with base Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}.

Proof.

The base map π0:M→N\pi_{0}:M\to N is a surjective submersion, and we may assume that 𝒢=π0∗​ℋ\mathcal{G}=\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H} and π=pr:π0∗​ℋ→ℋ\pi=\mathrm{pr}\colon\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}, (x,h,y)↦h(x,h,y)\mapsto h.

Let Hor0⊂T​M\operatorname{Hor}_{0}\subset TM be an Ehresmann connection for π0\pi_{0}. Note that

π¯0:=T​π0|Hor0:Hor0→T​N\overline{\pi}_{0}:=T\pi_{0}|_{\operatorname{Hor}_{0}}\colon\operatorname{Hor}_{0}\to TN

is a vector bundle map and a surjective submersion. Pulling back T​ℋ⇉T​NT\mathcal{H}\rightrightarrows TN along this map, we obtain the pullback 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid

Hor:=π¯0∗Tℋ=Hor0×T​𝒕π¯0Tℋ×π¯0T​𝒔Hor0⇉Hor0,\operatorname{Hor}:=\overline{\pi}_{0}^{*}T\mathcal{H}=\operatorname{Hor}_{0}{}_{\overline{\pi}_{0}}\times_{T\boldsymbol{t}}T\mathcal{H}{}_{T\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{\overline{\pi}_{0}}\operatorname{Hor}_{0}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0},

which is canonically a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid of T​(π0∗​ℋ)T(\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H}) and defines a MEC for pr\mathrm{pr}. Uniqueness follows because any MEC with base Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} would be a subset of Hor\operatorname{Hor}. ∎

4.4. Uniform Lie groupoid fibrations

For uniform Lie groupoid fibrations (recall Definition 2.1), the existence problem can be reduced to the case of extensions.

Proposition 4.5.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a uniform Lie groupoid fibration. If the induced map π∗:𝒢→π0∗​ℋ\pi^{*}\colon\mathcal{G}\to\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H} admits a MEC, then so does π\pi.

Proof.

We can factor π=pr∘π∗\pi=\mathrm{pr}\circ\pi^{*}, where pr:π0∗​ℋ→ℋ\mathrm{pr}:\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H} is a Morita fibration. By Proposition 4.4, pr\mathrm{pr} admits a MEC. The result follows from the lemma below. ∎

Lemma 4.6.

If the Lie groupoid surjective submersions π1:𝒢1→𝒢2\pi^{1}\colon\mathcal{G}^{1}\to\mathcal{G}^{2} and π2:𝒢2→𝒢3\pi^{2}\colon\mathcal{G}^{2}\to\mathcal{G}^{3} admit MECs, then so does their composition π2∘π1:𝒢1→𝒢3\pi^{2}\circ\pi^{1}\colon\mathcal{G}^{1}\to\mathcal{G}^{3}.

Proof.

Consider MECs Hor1⇉Hor01\operatorname{Hor}^{1}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}^{1}_{0} for π1\pi^{1} and Hor2⇉Hor02\operatorname{Hor}^{2}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}^{2}_{0} for π2\pi^{2}. Then

Hor:=Hor1∩(T​π1)−1​(Hor2)⇉Hor0:=Hor01∩(T​π01)−1​(Hor02)\operatorname{Hor}:=\operatorname{Hor}^{1}\cap(T\pi^{1})^{-1}(\operatorname{Hor}^{2})\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0}:=\operatorname{Hor}^{1}_{0}\cap(T\pi^{1}_{0})^{-1}(\operatorname{Hor}^{2}_{0})

is a MEC for π1∘π2\pi^{1}\circ\pi^{2}. Indeed, that Hor\operatorname{Hor} and Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} are Ehresmann connections is a general fact about Ehresmann connections for compositions of submersions. Multiplicativity follows because T​π1T\pi^{1} is a groupoid map. ∎

Proposition 4.5 together with Theorem 4.1 yield the following.

Corollary 4.7.

Any uniform Lie groupoid fibration π:𝒢→ℋ\pi:\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H}, defined on a proper Lie groupoid 𝒢\mathcal{G}, admits a multiplicative Ehresmann connection.

5. Completeness and the connection on the kernel

We turn to completeness of multiplicative Ehresmann connections. We prove two completeness criteria. The first generalises [12]*Proposition 2.17 to fibrations and is in terms of the connections induced on the kernel, while the second is in terms of the connection induced on the base.

We discuss first the connection induced on the kernel. Given a Lie groupoid surjective submersion π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H}, its kernel

𝒦:=ker⁡(π)=π−1​(𝒖​(N))⇉M\mathcal{K}:=\ker(\pi)=\pi^{-1}(\boldsymbol{u}(N))\rightrightarrows M

is a family of Lie groupoids with projection denoted

π¯:=𝒔∘π|𝒦:𝒦→N.\underline{\pi}:=\boldsymbol{s}\circ\pi|_{\mathrm{\mathcal{K}}}\colon\mathcal{K}\to N.

First, we note that MECs are inherited by kernels (for extensions, this was remarked in [20]*Section 4.1 and in [12]*Section 2.4).

Proposition 5.1.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a Lie groupoid surjective submersion. Given a MEC Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} for π\pi, we have that

Hor𝒦:=Hor∩T​𝒦⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}:=\operatorname{Hor}\cap T\mathcal{K}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0}

is a MEC on the kernel π¯:𝒦→N\underline{\pi}\colon\mathcal{K}\to N.

Proof.

We use the following general fact. Let f:A→Bf:A\to B be a surjective submersion, B′⊂BB^{\prime}\subset B an embedded submanifold, and A′:=f−1​(B′)A^{\prime}:=f^{-1}(B^{\prime}). Then A′A^{\prime} is an embedded submanifold, and f′:=f|A′:A′→B′f^{\prime}:=f|_{A^{\prime}}\colon A^{\prime}\to B^{\prime} is a surjective submersion. Moreover, if Hor\operatorname{Hor} is an Ehresmann connection for ff, then Hor′:=Hor∩T​A′\operatorname{Hor}^{\prime}:=\operatorname{Hor}\cap TA^{\prime} is an Ehresmann connection for f′f^{\prime} with corresponding horizontal lift given by restriction:

hor′=hor|T​B′×B′A′:T​B′×B′A′⊂T​B×BA→Hor′=Hor∩T​A′.\operatorname{hor}^{\prime}=\operatorname{hor}|_{TB^{\prime}\times_{B^{\prime}}A^{\prime}}\colon TB^{\prime}\times_{B^{\prime}}A^{\prime}\subset TB\times_{B}A\to\operatorname{Hor}^{\prime}=\operatorname{Hor}\cap\ TA^{\prime}.

Using this fact for f:=π:𝒢→ℋf:=\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} and B′:=𝒖​(N)B^{\prime}:=\boldsymbol{u}(N), we obtain that Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}} defined above is a connection for π′:𝒦→𝒖​(N)\pi^{\prime}\colon\mathcal{K}\to\boldsymbol{u}(N) (which is the map π¯\underline{\pi} from the statement, under the identification N≅𝒖​(N)N\cong\boldsymbol{u}(N)). Moreover, the horizontal lift restricts to a groupoid isomorphism:

hor𝒦:T​𝒖​(N)×𝒖​(N)𝒦→Hor𝒦,\operatorname{hor}^{\mathcal{K}}\colon T\boldsymbol{u}(N)\times_{\boldsymbol{u}(N)}\mathcal{K}\to\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}},

where Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}—as the intersection of two groupoids—is set-theoretically a groupoid. Using that hor\operatorname{hor} is a groupoid map covering hor0\operatorname{hor}_{0}, we have that:

T​𝒔​(hor⁡(T​𝒖​(v),k))=hor0⁡(v,𝒔​(k)),for allv∈Tx​N,k∈π−1​(𝒖​(x)).T\boldsymbol{s}(\operatorname{hor}(T\boldsymbol{u}(v),k))=\operatorname{hor}_{0}(v,\boldsymbol{s}(k)),\quad\textrm{for all}\quad v\in T_{x}N,\ k\in\pi^{-1}(\boldsymbol{u}(x)).

Since hor0⁡(⋅,𝒔​(k)):Tx​N→(Hor0)𝒔​(k)\operatorname{hor}_{0}(\cdot,\boldsymbol{s}(k))\colon T_{x}N\to(\operatorname{Hor}_{0})_{\boldsymbol{s}(k)} is a linear isomorphism, the equality shows that also T​𝒔:(Hor𝒦)k→(Hor0)s​(k)T\boldsymbol{s}\colon(\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}})_{k}\to(\operatorname{Hor}_{0})_{s(k)} is surjective. In particular, the base of Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}} is Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}. That Hor𝒦⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is indeed a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid of T​𝒦T\mathcal{K} follows now from Proposition B.1. ∎

The following result generalises [12]*Proposition 2.17 to fibrations.

Theorem 5.2.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a Lie groupoid fibration with kernel bundle π¯:𝒦:=ker⁡(π)→N\underline{\pi}\colon\mathcal{K}:=\ker(\pi)\to N. A multiplicative Ehresmann connection Hor\operatorname{Hor} for π\pi is complete if and only if the induced multiplicative Ehresmann connection Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}} on π¯\underline{\pi} is complete.

Proof.

Assume that Hor\operatorname{Hor} is a complete MEC for π\pi. Then for any δ∈C∞​(I,N)\delta\in C^{\infty}(I,N) and any k∈π−1​(𝒖​(δ​(0)))⊂𝒦k\in\pi^{-1}(\boldsymbol{u}(\delta(0)))\subset\mathcal{K}, the Hor\operatorname{Hor}-horizontal lift τ𝒖​(δ)t​(k)\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{u}(\delta)}(k) is defined on II. Since π∘τ𝒖​(δ)​(k)=𝒖​(δ)\pi\circ\tau_{\boldsymbol{u}(\delta)}(k)=\boldsymbol{u}(\delta), it follows that τ𝒖​(δ)t​(k)∈𝒦\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{u}(\delta)}(k)\in\mathcal{K}. Thus dd​t​τ𝒖​(δ)t​(k)∈T​𝒦∩Hor=Hor𝒦\frac{d}{dt}\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{u}(\delta)}(k)\in T\mathcal{K}\cap\operatorname{Hor}=\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}. So τ𝒖​(δ)t​(k)\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{u}(\delta)}(k) is also the Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}-horizontal lift of 𝒖​(δ)\boldsymbol{u}(\delta). This shows that Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}} is complete.

Conversely, assume that Hor\operatorname{Hor} is MEC for π\pi such that Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}} is complete. Note that Hor\operatorname{Hor} and Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}} induce the same connection Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} on π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N; simply because

Hor0=T​(𝒖​(M))∩Hor⊂T​𝒦∩Hor=Hor𝒦.\operatorname{Hor}_{0}=T(\boldsymbol{u}(M))\cap\operatorname{Hor}\subset T\mathcal{K}\cap\operatorname{Hor}=\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}.

By Proposition 3.6 completeness of Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}} implies completeness of Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}.

Consider a smooth path γ∈C∞​(I,ℋ)\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,\mathcal{H}) pick g∈π−1​(γ​(0))g\in\pi^{-1}(\gamma(0)). Assume γ\gamma cannot be lifted completely at gg, and consider its maximal lift

τγt​(g):[0,tm)→𝒢.\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g)\colon[0,{t_{\text{m}}})\to\mathcal{G}.

By completeness of Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}, the Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}-lift of 𝒔​(γ)\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma) at 𝒔​(g)\boldsymbol{s}(g) is defined on II, and by Proposition 3.5, for t∈[0,tm)t\in[0,{t_{\text{m}}}) it is given by

τ𝒔​(γ)t​(𝒔​(g))=𝒔​(τγt​(g)).\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}(\boldsymbol{s}(g))=\boldsymbol{s}(\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g)).

In particular, we can consider τ𝒔​(γ)tm​(𝒔​(g))∈M\tau^{{t_{\text{m}}}}_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}(\boldsymbol{s}(g))\in M. Since π\pi is a Lie groupoid fibration, there exists g′∈𝒢g^{\prime}\in\mathcal{G} such that:

π​(g′)=γ​(tm)and𝒔​(g′)=τ𝒔​(γ)tm​(𝒔​(g)).\pi(g^{\prime})=\gamma({t_{\text{m}}})\qquad\textrm{and}\qquad\boldsymbol{s}(g^{\prime})=\tau^{{t_{\text{m}}}}_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}(\boldsymbol{s}(g)).

Next, consider the Hor\operatorname{Hor}-lift of γ\gamma which at t=tmt={t_{\text{m}}} gives g′g^{\prime}:

τγ⋅,tm​(g′):(tm−δ,tm+δ)→𝒢,t↦τγt,tm​(g′),\tau^{\cdot,{t_{\text{m}}}}_{\gamma}(g^{\prime}):({t_{\text{m}}}-\delta,{t_{\text{m}}}+\delta)\to\mathcal{G},\qquad t\mapsto\tau^{t,{t_{\text{m}}}}_{\gamma}(g^{\prime}),

which is well-defined for some 0<δ<min​(tm,1−tm)0<\delta<\mathrm{min}({t_{\text{m}}},1-{t_{\text{m}}}). Using Proposition 3.5, this satisfies for all t∈(tm−δ,tm+δ)t\in({t_{\text{m}}}-\delta,{t_{\text{m}}}+\delta)

𝒔∘τγt,tm​(g′)=τ𝒔​(γ)t,tm​(𝒔​(g′))=τ𝒔​(γ)t,tm​(τ𝒔​(γ)tm​(𝒔​(g)))=τ𝒔​(γ)t​(𝒔​(g)),\boldsymbol{s}\circ\tau^{t,{t_{\text{m}}}}_{\gamma}(g^{\prime})=\tau_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}^{t,{t_{\text{m}}}}(\boldsymbol{s}(g^{\prime}))=\tau^{t,{t_{\text{m}}}}_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}\big(\tau^{{t_{\text{m}}}}_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}(\boldsymbol{s}(g))\big)=\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}(\boldsymbol{s}(g)),

where the last equality uses the multiplicativity property of parallel transport. We conclude that the following path is well-defined

η:(tm−δ,tm)→𝒢,η​(t):=τγt​(g)⋅(τγt,tm​(g′))−1.\eta\colon({t_{\text{m}}}-\delta,{t_{\text{m}}})\to\mathcal{G},\qquad\eta(t):=\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g)\cdot\big(\tau_{\gamma}^{t,{t_{\text{m}}}}(g^{\prime})\big)^{-1}.

Proposition 3.5 implies that η\eta is a horizontal path. It covers

π​(η​(t))=π​(τγt​(g))⋅π​(τγt,tm​(g′))−1=γ​(t)⋅γ−1​(t)=𝒖​(𝒕​(γ))​(t).\pi(\eta(t))=\pi(\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(g))\cdot\pi(\tau_{\gamma}^{t,{t_{\text{m}}}}(g^{\prime}))^{-1}=\gamma(t)\cdot\gamma^{-1}(t)=\boldsymbol{u}(\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma))(t).

Fix t0∈(tm−δ,tm)t_{0}\in({t_{\text{m}}}-\delta,{t_{\text{m}}}) and denote k0:=η​(t0)k_{0}:=\eta(t_{0}). By completeness of Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}, the Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}-horizontal lift of 𝒖∘𝒕​(γ)\boldsymbol{u}\circ\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma), which at t=t0t=t_{0} equals k0k_{0} is defined on II

τ𝒖∘𝒕​(γ)t,t0​(k0):I→𝒦.\tau^{t,t_{0}}_{\boldsymbol{u}\circ\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma)}(k_{0})\colon I\to\mathcal{K}.

By the uniqueness of horizontal lifts, we have that:

η​(t)=τ𝒖∘𝒕​(γ)t,t0​(k0),for ​t∈(tm−δ,tm).\eta(t)=\tau^{t,t_{0}}_{\boldsymbol{u}\circ\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma)}(k_{0}),\qquad\mbox{for }t\in({t_{\text{m}}}-\delta,{t_{\text{m}}}).

This implies smoothness of the path

ζ:[0,tm+δ)→𝒢,t↦{τγt​(g)if ​t∈[0,tm),τ𝒖​(𝒕​(γ))t,t0​(k0)⋅τγt,tm​(g′)if ​t∈(tm−δ,tm+δ).\zeta\colon[0,{t_{\text{m}}}+\delta)\to\mathcal{G},\qquad t\mapsto\begin{cases}\tau^{t}_{\gamma}(g)&\mbox{if }t\in[0,{t_{\text{m}}}),\\ \tau^{t,t_{0}}_{\boldsymbol{u}(\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma))}(k_{0})\cdot\tau^{t,{t_{\text{m}}}}_{\gamma}(g^{\prime})&\mbox{if }t\in({t_{\text{m}}}-\delta,{t_{\text{m}}}+\delta).\end{cases}

The fact that ζ\zeta is horizontal and covers γ\gamma contradicts the maximality of tm{t_{\text{m}}}.

We conclude that Hor\operatorname{Hor} is complete as well. ∎

In fact, we have proven a stronger result: the implication

Hor⁡ is complete ⟹Hor𝒦⁡ is complete \operatorname{Hor}\textrm{ is complete }\implies\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}\textrm{ is complete }

does not use the assumption that π\pi is a Lie groupoid fibration. However, if the base is source-connected, existence of a complete MEC already implies that we are dealing with a Lie groupoid fibration, as the following result shows.

Proposition 5.3.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a Lie groupoid surjective submersion which admits a complete MEC. If ℋ\mathcal{H} is source-connected, then π\pi is a Lie groupoid fibration.

Proof.

By Theorem A.1 it suffices to show that, for any x∈Mx\in M, π\pi restricts to a surjection between 𝒔\boldsymbol{s}-fibres π:𝒔−1​(x)→𝒔−1​(π0​(x))\pi\colon\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(x)\to\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(\pi_{0}(x)). Take some h∈𝒔−1​(π0​(x))h\in\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(\pi_{0}(x)). By assumption, there is a smooth path γ:I→𝒔−1​(π0​(x))\gamma\colon I\to\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(\pi_{0}(x)) such that γ​(0)=𝒖​(π0​(x))\gamma(0)=\boldsymbol{u}(\pi_{0}(x)) and γ​(1)=h\gamma(1)=h. By completeness, t↦τγt​(𝒖​(x))t\mapsto\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(\boldsymbol{u}(x)) is defined on II. Proposition 3.5 yields 𝒔∘τγt​(𝒖​(x))=τ𝒔​(γ)t​(x)=x\boldsymbol{s}\circ\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(\boldsymbol{u}(x))=\tau_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}^{t}(x)=x. Thus g:=τγ1​(𝒖​(x))∈𝒔−1​(x)g:=\tau_{\gamma}^{1}(\boldsymbol{u}(x))\in\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(x) and satisfies π​(g)=h\pi(g)=h. ∎

For later use, let us note the following consequence of the proof of Theorem 5.2.

Corollary 5.4.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a Lie groupoid surjective submersion which admits a complete MEC. Then π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N admits a complete Ehresmann connection.

The assumption that π\pi is a fibration was used for the proof of the implication

Hor𝒦⁡ is complete ⟹Hor⁡ is complete.\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}\textrm{ is complete }\implies\operatorname{Hor}\textrm{ is complete}.

The next example shows that the fibration assumption was indeed necessary.

Example 5.5.

Let ℋ=N×Γ⇉N\mathcal{H}=N\times\Gamma\rightrightarrows N be a constant family of groups, where Γ\Gamma is any discrete, non-trivial group. Fix x0∈Nx_{0}\in N and consider the open subgroupoid ℋ∗:=ℋ\({x0}×Γ\{eΓ})⇉N\mathcal{H}^{*}:=\mathcal{H}\backslash(\{x_{0}\}\times\Gamma\backslash\{e_{\Gamma}\})\rightrightarrows N. Consider the disjoint union Lie groupoid

(𝒢⇉N⊔N):=(ℋ⇉N)⊔(ℋ∗⇉N).\big(\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows N\sqcup N\big):=\big(\mathcal{H}\rightrightarrows N\big)\,\sqcup\,\big(\mathcal{H}^{*}\rightrightarrows N\big).

We have an induced Lie groupoid surjective submersion π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H}, which is the identity and inclusion on the respective factors. The map π\pi is not a Lie groupoid fibration, as, for g≠eΓg\neq e_{\Gamma}, the arrow (x0,g)(x_{0},g) cannot be lifted over the copy of x0x_{0} in the second factor.

The map π\pi is a local diffeomorphism, and the unique connection is multiplicative. However, π\pi is not a covering space, as the point (x0,eΓ)∈ℋ(x_{0},e_{\Gamma})\in\mathcal{H} is not evenly covered, and so the connection is not complete.

The kernel bundle is simply the unit groupoid 𝒦=N⊔N\mathcal{K}=N\sqcup N and the projection π¯:𝒦→N\underline{\pi}\colon\mathcal{K}\to N is a covering space. So it admits the path-lifting property and thus the induced connection on the kernel bundle is complete.  △\vartriangle

Next, using the fact that the unit component of a Lie groupoid is generated by an arbitrary open neighbourhood of the units, we show that if the kernel is a source-connected Lie groupoid, then the completeness problem can be reduced further to the base connection, providing a partial converse to Corollary 5.4.

Theorem 5.6.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a Lie groupoid fibration and suppose that the kernel is source-connected. A multiplicative Ehresmann connection Hor\operatorname{Hor} for π\pi is complete if and only if the induced connection Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} on the base submersion π0\pi_{0} is complete.

For this, we will use the following:

Lemma 5.7.

Let XX be a multiplicative vector field on 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M, which covers XMX_{M} on MM. If 𝒢\mathcal{G} is source-connected and XMX_{M} is complete, then so is XX.

Proof.

The set UU where ϕX1\phi_{X}^{1} (:=:= the flow of XX at time 11) is defined, is an open subset of 𝒢\mathcal{G}. Since XX is multiplicative, XX is tangent to the zero-section MM. Thus, its restriction to MM is the complete vector field XMX_{M} and M⊂UM\subset U. Multiplicativity implies that UU is a subgroupoid. The only open subgroupoid of a source-connected Lie groupoid that contains the units is the whole groupoid (see, for example [5]*Proposition 13.7). Hence XX is complete. ∎

Proof of Theorem 5.6.

By Theorem 5.2, it suffices to show that completeness of Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} implies that of Hor𝒦\operatorname{Hor}^{\mathcal{K}}. This is equivalent to the horizontal lift to 𝒦\mathcal{K} of any complete vector field X∈𝔛​(N)X\in\mathfrak{X}(N) be complete. Since XX is a multiplicative vector field on the unit groupoid, by Proposition 3.3 hor𝒦⁡(X)\operatorname{hor}^{\mathcal{K}}(X) is multiplicative, and it covers hor0⁡(X)\operatorname{hor}_{0}(X), which is complete because Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is. Hence, the previous lemma yields the result. ∎

The example below shows that source-connectedness is a necessary condition.

Example 5.8.

Consider a Lie groupoid, similar to the one in Example 5.5:

𝒢=(M×Γ)\({x0}×Γ\{eΓ})⇉M\mathcal{G}=(M\times\Gamma)\backslash(\{x_{0}\}\times\Gamma\backslash\{e_{\Gamma}\})\rightrightarrows M

where Γ\Gamma is any discrete, non-trivial group and x0∈Mx_{0}\in M. Then 𝒢\mathcal{G} is a bundle of Lie groups with projection π=pr1:𝒢→M\pi=\mathrm{pr}_{1}\colon\mathcal{G}\to M. Notice that π\pi is a local diffeomorphism, and, therefore, it admits only a unique connection Hor=0𝒢\operatorname{Hor}=0_{\mathcal{G}}, which is not complete as π\pi is not a covering map, but it is clearly multiplicative. Nevertheless, the base connection Hor0=0M\operatorname{Hor}_{0}=0_{M} on π0=idM\pi_{0}=\mathrm{id}_{M} is clearly complete.  △\vartriangle

For Morita fibrations, by using also Proposition 4.4, we obtain the following.

Corollary 5.9.

A Morita fibration π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} admits a complete MEC if and only if the base map π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N admits a complete Ehresmann connection.

Proof.

By Corollary 5.4, we know that the base Ehresmann connection of a complete MEC is complete as well.

Conversely, any Morita fibration is isomorphic to pullback pr:π0∗​ℋ→ℋ\mathrm{pr}:\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}, (x,h,y)↦h(x,h,y)\mapsto h. By Proposition 4.4, any connection Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} for π0\pi_{0} gives a unique MEC Hor\operatorname{Hor} for pr\mathrm{pr}. The explicit description of this correspondence from the proof of Proposition 4.4 implies that the Hor\operatorname{Hor}-horizontal lift of γ:I→ℋ\gamma\colon I\to\mathcal{H} is given by

t↦τγt​(x,γ​(0),y)=(τ𝒕​(γ)t​(x),γ​(t),τ𝒔​(γ)t​(y)),t\mapsto\tau_{\gamma}^{t}(x,\gamma(0),y)=(\tau_{\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma)}^{t}(x),\gamma(t),\tau^{t}_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)}(y)),

for any x∈π0−1​(𝒕​(γ)​(0))x\in\pi_{0}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma)(0)), y∈π0−1​(𝒕​(γ)​(0))y\in\pi_{0}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma)(0)), where τ𝒕​(γ)\tau_{\boldsymbol{t}(\gamma)} and τ𝒔​(γ)\tau_{\boldsymbol{s}(\gamma)} are Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0}-horizontal lifts. Thus, if Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is complete, then so is Hor\operatorname{Hor}. ∎

Next, for uniform Lie groupoid fibrations, we obtain a variant of Proposition 4.5 in the complete case, with the same strategy of proof.

Corollary 5.10.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a uniform Lie groupoid fibration. If π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N admits a complete Ehresmann connection and π∗:𝒢→π0∗​ℋ\pi^{*}\colon\mathcal{G}\to\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H} admits a complete MEC then π\pi admits a complete MEC.

The existence and completeness of multiplicative Ehresmann connections in the case of Lie groupoid extensions have been characterised in [12].

6. Families of Lie groupoids

A family of Lie groupoids is a Lie groupoid surjective submersion

π:(𝒢⇉M)→N,\pi\colon(\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M)\to N,

where NN is a manifold regarded as a unit groupoid. This yields indeed a family of Lie groupoids indexed by y∈Ny\in N:

𝒢y:=π−1​(y)⇉π0−1​(y),\mathcal{G}_{y}:=\pi^{-1}(y)\rightrightarrows\pi_{0}^{-1}(y),

where, as usually, π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N denotes the base map of π\pi.

In this section, we discuss in detail multiplicative Ehresmann connections for families, with focus on the existence and completeness problems.

We begin with a useful description of MECs in this setting.

Lemma 6.1.

An Ehresmann connection for a family of Lie groupoids is multiplicative if and only if the horizontal vector fields coincide with the multiplicative vector fields.

Proof.

Assume that Hor⇉Hor0\operatorname{Hor}\rightrightarrows\operatorname{Hor}_{0} is a MEC for π:(𝒢⇉M)→N\pi\colon(\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M)\to N. Then every vector field X∈𝔛​(N)X\in\mathfrak{X}(N) is a multiplicative vector field of a unit groupoid. Therefore, Proposition 3.3 implies that hor⁡(X)\operatorname{hor}(X) is multiplicative.

Conversely, let Hor\operatorname{Hor} be an Ehresmann connection for π:(𝒢⇉M)→N\pi\colon(\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M)\to N such that X𝒢:=hor⁡(X)X_{\mathcal{G}}:=\operatorname{hor}(X) is multiplicative, for all X∈𝔛​(N)X\in\mathfrak{X}(N). In particular, X𝒢X_{\mathcal{G}} is tangent to the unit section MM; we denote the induced vector field on MM by XMX_{M}. This shows that along MM, Hor|M⊂T​M\operatorname{Hor}|_{M}\subset TM, and so it defines an Ehresmann connection Hor0:=Hor|M\operatorname{Hor}_{0}:=\operatorname{Hor}|_{M} for π0\pi_{0} with horizontal lift X↦XMX\mapsto X_{M}. Since the groupoid morphism X𝒢:𝒢→T​𝒢X_{\mathcal{G}}\colon\mathcal{G}\to T\mathcal{G} covers XM:M→T​MX_{M}\colon M\to TM, the following compatibility relations hold:

T​𝒔∘X𝒢=XM∘𝒔andT​𝒕∘X𝒢=XM∘𝒕.T\boldsymbol{s}\circ X_{\mathcal{G}}=X_{M}\circ\boldsymbol{s}\quad\textrm{and}\quad T\boldsymbol{t}\circ X_{\mathcal{G}}=X_{M}\circ\boldsymbol{t}.

This implies that T​𝒔​(Hor)⊂Hor0T\boldsymbol{s}(\operatorname{Hor})\subset\operatorname{Hor}_{0} and T​𝒕​(Hor)⊂Hor0T\boldsymbol{t}(\operatorname{Hor})\subset\operatorname{Hor}_{0}. Consider now a composable pair (v,w)(v,w) in Horg×Horh\operatorname{Hor}_{g}\times\operatorname{Hor}_{h}. Denote x:=π​(g)=π​(h)x:=\pi(g)=\pi(h) and u0:=Tg​π​(v)=Th​π​(w)u_{0}:=T_{g}\pi(v)=T_{h}\pi(w). We extend u0u_{0} to a vector field XX on NN. Horizontality implies that v=X𝒢|gv=X_{\mathcal{G}}|_{g} and w=X𝒢|hw=X_{\mathcal{G}}|_{h}. Since v𝒢v^{\mathcal{G}} is multiplicative, it follows that

T(g,h)​𝒎​(u,v)=T(g,h)​𝒎​(X𝒢|g,X𝒢|h)=X𝒢|g​h∈Horg​h.T_{(g,h)}\boldsymbol{m}(u,v)=T_{(g,h)}\boldsymbol{m}(X_{\mathcal{G}}|_{g},X_{\mathcal{G}}|_{h})=X_{\mathcal{G}}|_{gh}\in\operatorname{Hor}_{gh}.

This shows that Hor\operatorname{Hor} is closed under multiplication. That Hor\operatorname{Hor} is also closed under inversion follows from T​ι​(X𝒢)=X𝒢∘ιT\iota(X_{\mathcal{G}})=X_{\mathcal{G}}\circ\iota. ∎

Existence and completeness of multiplicative connections for families of Lie groupoids are closely related to the local triviality of the family.

Definition 6.2.

The family of Lie groupoids π:𝒢→N\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to N is called locally trivial with typical fibre the Lie groupoid ℱ⇉F\mathcal{F}\rightrightarrows F, if NN admits an open cover {Uα}α∈Λ\{U^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda}, together with local trivializations that are groupoid isomorphisms:

,such thatpr1∘ψα=π|π−1​(Uα).\hbox to106.93pt{\vbox to55.66pt{\pgfpicture\makeatletter\hbox{\hskip 53.4669pt\lower-26.95946pt\hbox to0.0pt{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\nullfont\hbox to0.0pt{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{}{}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{{}}{{}}{{}}{{}}}{{{}}}{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{${{\pi_{0}^{-1}(U^{\alpha})}}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{
