}{}{}{}{}{{}}{}{}{{}}\pgfsys@moveto{-10.38037pt}{17.65973pt}\pgfsys@lineto{14.57655pt}{17.65973pt}\pgfsys@stroke\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}}}{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}{{{}}{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{14.77654pt}{17.65973pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}}\hbox{\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }{{{{}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{-2.88107pt}{21.3736pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{{\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\psi^{\alpha}}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }{{{{}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{-0.82693pt}{12.61842pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{{\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\sim}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{{}}{}{}{}{}{}{{{}{}}}{}{}{{{}{}}}\hbox{\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{{{}}}{{{}}}}{{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{{}}{}{}{}{}{}{{{}{}}}{}{}{{{}{}}}\hbox{\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{{{}}}{{{}}}}{{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{}} {}{}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@setlinewidth{\the\pgflinewidth}\pgfsys@invoke{ }{}{}{}{}{{}}{}{}{{}}\pgfsys@moveto{35.85435pt}{10.4667pt}\pgfsys@lineto{35.85435pt}{-9.70677pt}\pgfsys@stroke\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}}}{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}{{{}}{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{0.0}{-1.0}{1.0}{0.0}{35.85435pt}{-9.90675pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{{}}{}{}{}{}{}{{{}{}}}{}{}{{{}{}}}\hbox{\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{{{}}}{{{}}}} {{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} }\pgfsys@setlinewidth{\the\pgflinewidth}\pgfsys@invoke{ }{}{}{}{}{{}}{}{}{{}}\pgfsys@moveto{31.03215pt}{10.4667pt}\pgfsys@lineto{31.03215pt}{-9.70677pt}\pgfsys@stroke\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}}}{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}{{{}}{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{0.0}{-1.0}{1.0}{0.0}{31.03215pt}{-9.90675pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{ }{}{}{}{}{{}}{}{}{{}}\pgfsys@moveto{-11.7276pt}{-18.29976pt}\pgfsys@lineto{12.81964pt}{-18.29976pt}\pgfsys@stroke\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}}}{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}{{{}}{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{13.01962pt}{-18.29976pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}}\hbox{\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }{{{{}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{-4.43314pt}{-14.58589pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{{\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\psi^{\alpha}_{0}}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }{{{{}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{-2.379pt}{-23.34106pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{{\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\sim}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{}{}{}\hss}\pgfsys@discardpath\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope\hss}}\endpgfpicture}},\quad\textrm{such that}\quad\mathrm{pr}_{1}\circ\psi^{\alpha}=\pi|_{\pi^{-1}(U^{\alpha})}.

 ▽\triangledown

6.1. Existence

The following existence result will be refined later on to incorporate completeness as well.

Proposition 6.3.

Any locally trivial family of Lie groupoids admits a MEC.

Proof.

Let {(Uα,ψα)}α∈Λ\{(U^{\alpha},\psi^{\alpha})\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda} be a trivialising cover. We use the local trivialization to obtain for each α∈Λ\alpha\in\Lambda a MEC on π−1​(Uα)\pi^{-1}(U^{\alpha}) with horizontal lift

horα:π∗​T​Uα→T​π−1​(Uα),(x,u)↦(Tx​ψα)−1​(u,0).\operatorname{hor}^{\alpha}\colon\pi^{*}TU^{\alpha}\to T\pi^{-1}(U^{\alpha}),\qquad(x,u)\mapsto(T_{x}\psi^{\alpha})^{-1}(u,0).

Consider a partition of unity {χα}α∈Λ\{\chi^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda} on NN subordinate to the cover {Uα}α∈Λ\{U^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda}. The pullbacks {π∗​χα}α∈Λ\{\pi^{*}\chi^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda} define a biinvariant partition of unity on 𝒢\mathcal{G} which is subordinate to {π−1​(Uα)}α∈Λ\{\pi^{-1}(U^{\alpha})\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda}. We glue the horizontal lifts horα\operatorname{hor}^{\alpha} using this partition of unity to obtain hor:=∑α∈Λπ∗​χα⋅horα\operatorname{hor}:=\sum_{\alpha\in\Lambda}\pi^{*}\chi^{\alpha}\cdot\operatorname{hor}^{\alpha}, a horizontal lift corresponding to a multiplicative Ehresmann connection for π\pi. ∎

Any family of Lie groupoids π:(𝒢⇉M)→N\pi\colon(\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M)\to N with π\pi proper is locally trivial (see [6]*Theorem 7.8 and [10]*Theorem 5.0.7); therefore, it admits a MEC. The techniques developed in [6] imply an even stronger version of this result.

Theorem 6.4.

Any family of Lie groupoids π:(𝒢⇉M)→N\pi\colon(\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M)\to N, where 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M is a proper Lie groupoid, admits a MEC.

When NN is an interval, the result was proven in [6]*Lemma 6.5 using the notion of deformation cohomology, which is developed in that paper, together with explicit homotopy operators for the deformation cohomology of proper Lie groupoids. The same proof strategy applies in our case. For the convenience of the reader, we present a self-contained proof that avoids the explicit use of deformation cohomology and instead extracts only the minimal tools from [6]. Nevertheless, [6] remains indispensable for a deeper understanding of the phenomenon.

As in [9, 6], we will use that on a proper Lie groupoid 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M one can build linear averaging operators along the 𝒕\boldsymbol{t}-fibres:

(6) C∞​(𝒕−1​(x))→ℝ,f↦∫𝒕−1​(x)f​(h)​𝑑h=not.∫xf​(h)​𝑑h,C^{\infty}(\boldsymbol{t}^{-1}(x))\to\mathbb{R},\qquad f\mapsto\int_{\boldsymbol{t}^{-1}(x)}f(h)dh\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mathrm{not.}}}{{=}}\int_{x}f(h)dh,

which are normalized: ∫x𝑑h=1\int_{x}dh=1, left-invariant: ∫𝒔​(g)f​(g​h)​𝑑h=∫𝒕​(g)f​(h)​𝑑h\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}f(gh)dh=\int_{\boldsymbol{t}(g)}f(h)dh, and smooth: if f∈C∞​(𝒢)f\in C^{\infty}(\mathcal{G}) then x↦∫xfx\mapsto\int_{x}f is in C∞​(M)C^{\infty}(M). These can be used to make vector fields multiplicative.

Lemma 6.5.

Let 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M be a proper Lie groupoid with averaging operators along the 𝐭\boldsymbol{t}-fibres (6). Let X∈𝔛​(𝒢)X\in\mathfrak{X}(\mathcal{G}) be an 𝐬\boldsymbol{s}-projectable vector field. Then

g↦X^g:=∫𝒔​(g)T​𝒎​(Xg​h,T​𝒊​(Xh))​𝑑h∈Tg​𝒢,g\mapsto\widehat{X}_{g}:=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{gh},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})\big)dh\in T_{g}\mathcal{G},

defines a multiplicative vector field X^\widehat{X} on 𝒢\mathcal{G}.

Proof.

That XX is 𝒔\boldsymbol{s}-projectable is crucial for T​𝒎​(Xg​h,T​𝒊​(Xh))T\boldsymbol{m}(X_{gh},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})) to be defined. Let us check the multiplicativity conditions now.

First, we calculate X^\widehat{X} along the unit section using the inverse axiom on T​𝒢T\mathcal{G}

X^𝒖​(x)=∫xT𝒎(Xh,T𝒊(Xh))dh=∫xT𝒕(Xh)dh=:Vx∈TxM.\widehat{X}_{\boldsymbol{u}(x)}=\int_{x}T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{h},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})\big)dh=\int_{x}T\boldsymbol{t}(X_{h})dh=:V_{x}\in T_{x}M.

Hence X^∘u=V\widehat{X}\circ u=V, where V∈𝔛​(M)V\in\mathfrak{X}(M) is a vector field.

Next, we note that X^\widehat{X} is 𝒔\boldsymbol{s}-projectable to VV

T​𝒔​(X^g)=∫𝒔​(g)T​𝒔∘T​𝒎​(Xg​h,T​𝒊​(Xh))​𝑑h=∫𝒔​(g)T​𝒕​(Xh)​𝑑h=V𝒔​(g).T\boldsymbol{s}(\widehat{X}_{g})=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}T\boldsymbol{s}\circ T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{gh},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})\big)dh=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}T\boldsymbol{t}(X_{h})dh=V_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}.

Using left-invariance, we obtain that X^\widehat{X} is also 𝒕\boldsymbol{t}-projectable to VV

T​𝒕​(X^g)\displaystyle T\boldsymbol{t}(\widehat{X}_{g}) =∫𝒔​(g)T​𝒕∘T​𝒎​(Xg​h,T​𝒊​(Xh))​𝑑h=∫𝒔​(g)T​𝒕​(Xg​h)​𝑑h\displaystyle=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}T\boldsymbol{t}\circ T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{gh},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})\big)dh=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}T\boldsymbol{t}(X_{gh})dh
=∫𝒕​(g)T​𝒕​(Xh)​𝑑h=V𝒕​(g)\displaystyle=\int_{\boldsymbol{t}(g)}T\boldsymbol{t}(X_{h})dh=V_{\boldsymbol{t}(g)}

To show multiplicativity, note that 𝒔\boldsymbol{s}- and 𝒕\boldsymbol{t}-projectability of X^\widehat{X} imply that, for any composable pair (g1,g2)∈𝒢(2)(g_{1},g_{2})\in\mathcal{G}^{(2)}, the pair (X^g1,X^g2)(\widehat{X}_{g_{1}},\widehat{X}_{g_{2}}) is also composable, i.e. it belongs to (T​𝒢)(2)(T\mathcal{G})^{(2)}. Using this and left-invariance, we obtain

T​𝒎​(X^g1,X^g2)\displaystyle T\boldsymbol{m}(\widehat{X}_{g_{1}},\widehat{X}_{g_{2}}) =T​𝒎​(∫𝒔​(g1)T​𝒎​(Xg1​h,T​𝒊​(Xh))​𝑑h,∫𝒔​(g2)T​𝒎​(Xg2​h,T​𝒊​(Xh))​𝑑h)\displaystyle=T\boldsymbol{m}\Big(\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g_{1})}T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{g_{1}h},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})\big)dh,\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g_{2})}T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{g_{2}h},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})\big)dh\Big)
=T​𝒎​(∫𝒔​(g1)T​𝒎​(Xg1​h,T​𝒊​(Xh))​𝑑h,∫𝒔​(g1)T​𝒎​(Xh,T​𝒊​(Xg2−1​h))​𝑑h),\displaystyle=T\boldsymbol{m}\Big(\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g_{1})}T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{g_{1}h},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})\big)dh,\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g_{1})}T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{h},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{g_{2}^{-1}h})\big)dh\Big),
and using linearity of T​𝒎T\boldsymbol{m}, we can further write
=∫𝒔​(g1)T​𝒎​(T​𝒎​(Xg1​h,T​𝒊​(Xh)),T​𝒎​(Xh,T​𝒊​(Xg2−1​h)))​𝑑h\displaystyle=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g_{1})}T\boldsymbol{m}\Big(T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{g_{1}h},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})\big),T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{h},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{g_{2}^{-1}h})\big)\Big)dh
=∫𝒔​(g1)T​𝒎​(Xg1​h,T​𝒊​(Xg2−1​h))​𝑑h\displaystyle=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g_{1})}T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{g_{1}h},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{g_{2}^{-1}h})\big)dh
=∫𝒔​(g2)T​𝒎​(Xg1​g2​h,T​𝒊​(Xh))​𝑑h=X^g1​g2.\displaystyle=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g_{2})}T\boldsymbol{m}\big(X_{g_{1}g_{2}h},T\boldsymbol{i}(X_{h})\big)dh=\widehat{X}_{g_{1}g_{2}}.

Compatibility with inversion follows from the other axioms. ∎

Proof of Theorem 6.4.

We start with an Ehresmann connection Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} on π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N with horizontal lift hor0:𝔛​(N)→𝔛​(M)\operatorname{hor}_{0}\colon\mathfrak{X}(N)\to\mathfrak{X}(M). Consider a second Ehresmann connection Hor𝒔\operatorname{Hor}_{\boldsymbol{s}} for the submersion 𝒔:𝒢→M\boldsymbol{s}\colon\mathcal{G}\to M with horizontal lift hor𝒔:𝔛​(M)→𝔛​(𝒢)\operatorname{hor}_{\boldsymbol{s}}\colon\mathfrak{X}(M)\to\mathfrak{X}(\mathcal{G}). For π\pi we obtain an induced Ehresmann connection Hor~:=Hor𝒔∩T​π−1​(Hor0)\widetilde{\operatorname{Hor}}:=\operatorname{Hor}_{\boldsymbol{s}}\cap T\pi^{-1}(\operatorname{Hor}_{0}) with horizontal lift hor~=hor𝒔∘hor0:𝔛​(N)→𝔛​(𝒢)\widetilde{\operatorname{hor}}=\operatorname{hor}_{\boldsymbol{s}}\circ\operatorname{hor}_{0}\colon\mathfrak{X}(N)\to\mathfrak{X}(\mathcal{G}). Note that hor~​(X)\widetilde{\operatorname{hor}}(X) is 𝒔\boldsymbol{s}-projectable to hor0⁡(X)\operatorname{hor}_{0}(X). We use the previous lemma to make the vector field hor~​(X)\widetilde{\operatorname{hor}}(X) multiplicative, hence we define:

hor:𝔛​(N)→𝔛mult​(𝒢),hor⁡(X):=hor~​(X)^.\operatorname{hor}\colon\mathfrak{X}(N)\to\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{mult}}(\mathcal{G}),\qquad\operatorname{hor}(X):=\widehat{\widetilde{\operatorname{hor}}(X)}.

Since π∘𝒎​(g,h)=π​(g)\pi\circ\boldsymbol{m}(g,h)=\pi(g), we have that

Tg​π​(hor⁡(X))\displaystyle T_{g}\pi(\operatorname{hor}(X)) =∫𝒔​(g)T​π∘T​𝒎​(hor~​(X)g​h,T​𝒊​(hor~​(X)h))​𝑑h\displaystyle=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}T\pi\circ T\boldsymbol{m}\big(\widetilde{\operatorname{hor}}(X)_{gh},T\boldsymbol{i}(\widetilde{\operatorname{hor}}(X)_{h})\big)dh
=∫𝒔​(g)T​π​(hor~​(X)g​h)​𝑑h=∫𝒔​(g)Xx​𝑑h=Xx,\displaystyle=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}T\pi(\widetilde{\operatorname{hor}}(X)_{gh})dh=\int_{\boldsymbol{s}(g)}X_{x}dh=X_{x},

where x=π​(g)=π​(g​h)∈Nx=\pi(g)=\pi(gh)\in N. Hence hor⁡(X)\operatorname{hor}(X) is π\pi-projectable to XX. As the fibres of π\pi are 𝒢\mathcal{G}-invariant, the construction implies that X↦hor⁡(X)X\mapsto\operatorname{hor}(X) is C∞​(N)C^{\infty}(N)-linear, and so it corresponds to the lift of an Ehresmann connection Hor\operatorname{Hor} for π\pi. Lemma 6.1 implies that Hor\operatorname{Hor} is multiplicative. ∎

Remark.

In [6]*Remark 7.2, it is shown, by means of an example, that for a family of Lie groupoids π:𝒢→N\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to N, the condition that 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M be a proper (or even source-proper) Lie groupoid does not imply local triviality of the family. The example, which relies on earlier work of Palais and Richardson, consists of a smooth family of actions ρt\rho_{t} of a compact Lie group GG on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n}, depending smoothly on t∈ℝt\in\mathbb{R}. Viewed as a family of Lie groupoids 𝒢=(G⋉ℝn)×ℝ\mathcal{G}=(G\ltimes\mathbb{R}^{n})\times\mathbb{R} over ℝ\mathbb{R}, it is not locally trivial around 0. Nevertheless, Theorem 6.4 still yields a MEC for this family.  △\vartriangle

6.2. Completeness

We recall Theorem 5.6 in the case of families.

Corollary 6.6.

For a source-connected family of Lie groupoids π:𝒢→N\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to N, a multiplicative Ehresmann connection Hor\operatorname{Hor} is complete if and only if the induced connection Hor0\operatorname{Hor}_{0} on the base is complete.

6.3. Existence of complete connections

A surjective submersion π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N is a locally trivial fibration if and only if it admits a complete Ehresmann connection. Somewhat surprisingly, this “classical” result was only recently given a correct proof by del Hoyo [11].

In this subsection, we discuss the relation between the existence of complete multiplicative connections and local triviality of a family of Lie groupoids, in the sense of Definition 6.2. Clearly, if a family admits a complete MEC, then its parallel transport yields local groupoid trivializations. Conversely, if π\pi is a locally trivial family, then, as seen in Proposition 6.3, it admits a MEC—which is, however, not guaranteed to be complete.

The main result of this section, which generalizes [11], is the following criterion for the existence of complete connections on locally trivial bundles.

Theorem 6.7.

A locally trivial family of Lie groupoids whose typical fibre is a source-proper Lie groupoid admits a complete MEC.

The proof mimics and extends the techniques developed in [11]. First, we give a geometric criterion for completeness of connections.

Lemma 6.8.

Let p:A→Bp\colon A\to B be a locally trivial fibre bundle with typical fibre FF, endowed with an Ehresmann connection Hor\operatorname{Hor}. If there exists a trivialising cover

{ψα:p−1​(Uα)→∼Uα×F}α∈Λ\{\psi^{\alpha}\colon p^{-1}(U^{\alpha})\xrightarrow{\raisebox{-1.68887pt}[0.0pt][0.0pt]{\smash{$\sim$}}}U^{\alpha}\times F\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda}

and, for each α∈Λ\alpha\in\Lambda, a closed subset Sα⊂FS^{\alpha}\subset F such that:

  1. (1)

    T​ψα​(Hor)|Uα×Sα=T​Uα×0SαT\psi^{\alpha}(\operatorname{Hor})|_{U^{\alpha}\times S^{\alpha}}=TU^{\alpha}\times 0_{S^{\alpha}} and

  2. (2)

    the connected components of F\SαF\backslash S^{\alpha} are precompact,

then Hor\operatorname{Hor} is complete.

Proof.

Assume first that A=B×FA=B\times F. Consider a connection Hor\operatorname{Hor} for the projection pr1:B×F→B\mathrm{pr}_{1}\colon B\times F\to B, and a closed subset S⊂FS\subset F satisfying the above conditions. Let η:I→B\eta\colon I\to B be a curve. For any x∈Fx\in F, the horizontal lift of η\eta starting at (η​(0),x)(\eta(0),x) has the form τηt​(η​(0),x)=(η​(t),ξ​(x,t))\tau^{t}_{\eta}(\eta(0),x)=(\eta(t),\xi(x,t)). Note that x↦ξ​(x,t)x\mapsto\xi(x,t) is the flow of the time dependent vector field X​(⋅,t)X(\cdot,t) on FF defined by the relation

hor(η​(t),x)⁡(dd​t​η​(t))=(dd​t​η​(t),X​(x,t)).\operatorname{hor}_{(\eta(t),x)}\big(\tfrac{d}{dt}\eta(t)\big)=\big(\tfrac{d}{dt}\eta(t),X(x,t)\big).

To show completeness of Hor\operatorname{Hor}, we need to show that, for any x∈Fx\in F, the flow line t↦ξ​(x,t)t\mapsto\xi(x,t) of XX is defined on II. This follows as we are in either of two cases:

  • •

    If x∈Sx\in S, then by (1) X​(x,t)=0X(x,t)=0 for all t∈It\in I, so t↦ξ​(x,t)=xt\mapsto\xi(x,t)=x is the flow line through xx, which is defined on II.

  • •

    If x∉Sx\notin S, then ξ​(x,t)\xi(x,t) stays within the connected component of xx in F\SF\backslash S, denoted Conn⁡(F\S,x)\operatorname{Conn}(F\backslash S,x). Indeed, otherwise there is t0>0t_{0}>0 such that ξ​(x,t0)=x0∈S\xi(x,t_{0})=x_{0}\in S, and so t↦x0t\mapsto x_{0} is a integral curve of vv that coincides with ξ​(x,t)\xi(x,t) at time t=t0t=t_{0}, therefore they must be equal, which yields the contradiction: x=x0∈Sx=x_{0}\in S. Hence, ξ​(x,t)\xi(x,t) must stay within the compact set Conn⁡(F\S,x)¯\overline{\operatorname{Conn}(F\backslash S,x)}, which implies that it is defined on the whole of II.

The general case of a locally trivial fibre bundle follows by using the above argument along a finite cover of a path by local trivialisations. ∎

Lemma 6.9.

Let π:(𝒢⇉M)→N\pi\colon(\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M)\to N be a locally trivial family of Lie groupoids with typical fibre a source-proper Lie groupoid ℱ⇉F\mathcal{F}\rightrightarrows F. Suppose that there exists

  • •

    an open cover by groupoid trivialisations {(Vα,ψα)}α∈Λ\{(V^{\alpha},\psi^{\alpha})\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda},

    ,withpr1∘ψα=π|π−1​(Vα),\hbox to107.74pt{\vbox to56.06pt{\pgfpicture\makeatletter\hbox{\hskip 53.87099pt\lower-27.15941pt\hbox to0.0pt{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@setlinewidth{\the\pgflinewidth}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\nullfont\hbox to0.0pt{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{}{}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{{}}{{}}{{}}{{}}}{{{}}}{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{-17.20648pt}{0.0pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{{\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{${{\pi^{-1}(V^{\alpha})}}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{}}}&\hskip 21.51202pt\hfil&\hfil\hskip 42.86862pt\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} }\hbox{{\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{${{V^{\alpha}\times\mathcal{F}}}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}&\qquad\hfil\cr\vskip 18.39993pt\cr\hfil\hskip 20.1648pt\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }{{{{}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{${{\pi_{0}^{-1}(V^{\alpha})}}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}&\hskip 20.1648pt\hfil&\hfil\hskip 44.62553pt\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} }\hbox{{\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{${{V^{\alpha}\times F}}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}&\qquad\hfil\cr}}}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}{{{{}}}{{}}{{}}{{}}{{}}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\psi^{\alpha}}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }{{{{}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\sim}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{ {}{}{}}{}{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{{{}}}{{{}}}}{{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{}} {}{}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{{}}{}{}{}{}{}{{{}{}}}{}{}{{{}{}}}\hbox{\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{{{}}}{{{}}}} {{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{}} {}{}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@setlinewidth{\the\pgflinewidth}\pgfsys@invoke{ }{}{}{}{}{{}}{}{}{{}}\pgfsys@moveto{-34.77007pt}{8.99997pt}\pgfsys@lineto{-34.77007pt}{-8.6pt}\pgfsys@stroke\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}}}{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}{{{}}{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{0.0}{-1.0}{1.0}{0.0}{-34.77007pt}{-8.79999pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{{}}{}{}{}{}{}{{{}{}}}{}{}{{{}{}}}\hbox{\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{{{}}}{{{}}}}{{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{}} {}{}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@setlinewidth{\the\pgflinewidth}\pgfsys@invoke{ }{}{}{}{}{{}}{}{}{{}}\pgfsys@moveto{36.1897pt}{10.66666pt}\pgfsys@lineto{36.1897pt}{-9.90671pt}\pgfsys@stroke\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}}}{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}{{{}}{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{0.0}{-1.0}{1.0}{0.0}{36.1897pt}{-10.10669pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{{}}{}{}{}{}{}{{{}{}}}{}{}{{{}{}}}\hbox{\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{{{}}}{{{}}}} {{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{{{}}{{}}}{}{{}}\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} {{}}{}{{}}{}{{}} {}{}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@setlinewidth{\the\pgflinewidth}\pgfsys@invoke{ }{}{}{}{}{{}}{}{}{{}}\pgfsys@moveto{31.3675pt}{10.66666pt}\pgfsys@lineto{31.3675pt}{-9.90671pt}\pgfsys@stroke\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}}}{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}{{{}}{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{0.0}{-1.0}{1.0}{0.0}{31.3675pt}{-10.10669pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{}{}{ {}{}{}}{}{ {}{}{}} {{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{}{{{{{}}{ {}{}}{}{}{{}{}}}}}{{}}{}{}{}{}{}{{{}{}}}{}{{}}{}{}{}{{{}{}}}{}{}{{{}{}}}{}{{}}{}{}{}{{{}{}}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@setlinewidth{\the\pgflinewidth}\pgfsys@invoke{ }{}{}{}{}{{}}{}{}{{}}\pgfsys@moveto{-11.99417pt}{-18.4997pt}\pgfsys@lineto{13.08623pt}{-18.4997pt}\pgfsys@stroke\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}}}{{}{{}}{}{}{{}}{{{}}{{{}}{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{13.28621pt}{-18.4997pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}{{}}}}\hbox{\hbox{\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }{{{{}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{-4.43314pt}{-14.78583pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{{\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\psi^{\alpha}_{0}}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}}\hbox{{\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }{{}{}{{ {}{}}}{ {}{}} {{}{{}}}{{}{}}{}{{}{}} { }{{{{}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{-2.379pt}{-23.541pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{{\definecolor{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@rgb@stroke{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@color@rgb@fill{0}{0}{0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\sim}$} }}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}}} \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{}{}{}\hss}\pgfsys@discardpath\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope\hss}}\endpgfpicture}},\quad\textrm{with}\quad\mathrm{pr}_{1}\circ\psi^{\alpha}=\pi|_{\pi^{-1}(V^{\alpha})},
  • •

    a locally finite open cover {Uα}α∈Λ\{U^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda}, with U¯α⊂Vα\overline{U}^{\alpha}\subset V^{\alpha},

  • •

    and, for each α∈Λ\alpha\in\Lambda, a closed, ℱ\mathcal{F}-invariant subset Sα⊂FS^{\alpha}\subset F such that

    • –

      the connected components of F\SαF\backslash S^{\alpha} are precompact,

    • –

      and, denoting Zα:=(ψ0α)−1​(U¯α×Sα)Z^{\alpha}:=(\psi_{0}^{\alpha})^{-1}(\overline{U}^{\alpha}\times S^{\alpha}), such that

      Zα∩Zβ=∅for allα≠β∈Λ.Z^{\alpha}\cap Z^{\beta}=\emptyset\qquad\textrm{for all}\quad\alpha\neq\beta\in\Lambda.

Then π\pi admits a complete MEC.

Proof.

Clearly also {U¯α}α∈Λ\{\overline{U}^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda} is locally finite. Then {Zα}α∈Λ\{Z^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda} is a locally finite family of 𝒢\mathcal{G}-invariant, disjoint, closed subsets in MM. This implies that the subsets

Wα:=π0−1​(Vα)\⋃β≠αZβW^{\alpha}:=\pi_{0}^{-1}(V^{\alpha})\backslash\bigcup_{\beta\neq\alpha}Z^{\beta}

are open [25]*Lemma 39.1 (c). Additionally, the family {Wα}α∈Λ\{W^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda} covers MM:

  • •

    if x∈M\⋃βZβx\in M\backslash\bigcup_{\beta}Z^{\beta}, since xx lies in π0−1​(Vα)\pi_{0}^{-1}(V^{\alpha}) for some α\alpha, we have that x∈Wαx\in W^{\alpha};

  • •

    since Zα⊂WαZ^{\alpha}\subset W^{\alpha}, we have that ⋃β∈ΛZβ⊂⋃β∈ΛWβ\bigcup_{\beta\in\Lambda}Z^{\beta}\subset\bigcup_{\beta\in\Lambda}W^{\beta}.

Clearly, each WαW^{\alpha} is 𝒢\mathcal{G}-invariant.

Since 𝒢⇉M\mathcal{G}\rightrightarrows M is a proper Lie groupoid, by [7]*Proposition 8 there exists a 𝒢\mathcal{G}-invariant partition of unity {χα}α∈Λ\{\chi^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda} on MM subordinate to the cover {Wα}α∈Λ\{W^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda}.

For each α\alpha, the trivialization induces a MEC on π−1​(Vα)\pi^{-1}(V^{\alpha}) with horizontal lift

horα:π∗​T​Vα→T​π−1​(Vα),horgα⁡(v)=(Tg​ψα)−1​(v,0).\operatorname{hor}^{\alpha}\colon\pi^{*}TV^{\alpha}\to T\pi^{-1}(V^{\alpha}),\qquad\operatorname{hor}_{g}^{\alpha}(v)=(T_{g}\psi^{\alpha})^{-1}(v,0).

We glue these connections using the partition of unity {χα∘𝒔}α∈Λ\{\chi^{\alpha}\circ\boldsymbol{s}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda}, which is subordinate to the cover {π−1​(Vα)}α∈Λ\{\pi^{-1}(V^{\alpha})\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda}, and obtain a connection Hor\operatorname{Hor} for π:𝒢→N\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to N with horizontal lift

hor:π∗​T​N→T​𝒢,hor=∑α∈Λχα∘𝒔⋅horα.\operatorname{hor}\colon\pi^{*}TN\to T\mathcal{G},\qquad\operatorname{hor}=\sum_{\alpha\in\Lambda}\chi^{\alpha}\circ\boldsymbol{s}\cdot\operatorname{hor}^{\alpha}.

Since the partition of unity is 𝒢\mathcal{G}-invariant, it follows that Hor\operatorname{Hor} is multiplicative.

To prove completeness of Hor\operatorname{Hor}, we check the two assumptions of Lemma 6.8 for the closed subsets 𝒔−1​(Sα)⊂ℱ\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(S^{\alpha})\subset\mathcal{F}.

  1. (1)

    Let g∈(ψα)−1​(Uα×𝒔−1​(Sα))g\in(\psi^{\alpha})^{-1}(U^{\alpha}\times\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(S^{\alpha})). Then 𝒔​(g)∈Zα\boldsymbol{s}(g)\in Z^{\alpha}, and so 𝒔​(g)∉Wβ\boldsymbol{s}(g)\notin W^{\beta}, for all β≠α\beta\neq\alpha. Therefore χβ​(𝒔​(g))=0\chi^{\beta}(\boldsymbol{s}(g))=0, for all β≠α\beta\neq\alpha. This implies that horg=horgα\operatorname{hor}_{g}=\operatorname{hor}^{\alpha}_{g}. This is equivalent to the second condition.

  2. (2)

    A connected component CC of ℱ\𝒔−1​(Sα)\mathcal{F}\backslash\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(S^{\alpha}) is sent by 𝒔\boldsymbol{s} into a connected component C0C_{0} of F\SαF\backslash S^{\alpha}. Now 𝒔\boldsymbol{s} is proper, and C0C_{0} is precompact, so CC is also precompact. Hence, the first condition holds. ∎

In [11], the subsets SαS^{\alpha} were constructed by using an exhaustion function. We will use such a function, which is invariant, as provided by the following result.

Proposition 6.10.

Let ℱ⇉F\mathcal{F}\rightrightarrows F be a source-proper Lie groupoid. There exists a smooth, proper function f:F→[0,∞)f:F\to[0,\infty) which is ℱ\mathcal{F}-invariant, i.e., 𝐬∗​f=𝐭∗​f\boldsymbol{s}^{*}f=\boldsymbol{t}^{*}f.

Proof.

Let q:F→F/ℱq\colon F\to F/\mathcal{F} be the quotient map to the orbit space. By [7]*Proposition 9, F/ℱF/\mathcal{F} admits a proper map f0:F/ℱ→[0,∞)f_{0}\colon F/\mathcal{F}\to[0,\infty) whose lift f=f0∘qf=f_{0}\circ q is smooth. Invariance of ff holds by construction; properness by the following. ∎

Lemma 6.11.

For any source-proper Lie groupoid ℱ⇉F\mathcal{F}\rightrightarrows F, the quotient map to the orbit-space q:F→F/ℱq\colon F\to F/\mathcal{F} is proper.

Proof.

First remark that the orbit of any x∈Fx\in F is compact, as it is given by 𝒪x=𝒕​(𝒔−1​(x))\mathscr{O}_{x}=\boldsymbol{t}(\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(x)). Next, note that any orbit 𝒪x\mathscr{O}_{x} has pre-compact open neighbourhoods: indeed, if U~\widetilde{U} is a precompact neighbourhood of 𝒪x\mathscr{O}_{x}, then its saturation U=𝒕​(𝒔−1​(U~))U=\boldsymbol{t}(\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(\widetilde{U})) is an invariant precompact neighbourhood.

Consider a compact subset K⊂F/ℱK\subset F/\mathcal{F}. For any 𝒪∈K\mathscr{O}\in K pick a precompact, invariant neighbourhood 𝒪⊂U𝒪⊂M\mathscr{O}\subset U_{\mathscr{O}}\subset M. The open cover {U𝒪/ℱ}𝒪∈K\{U_{\mathscr{O}}/\mathcal{F}\}_{\mathscr{O}\in K} admits a a finite open subcover {U𝒪i/𝒢}i=1n\{U_{\mathscr{O}_{i}}/\mathcal{G}\}_{i=1}^{n}. Then the closed set q−1​(K)q^{-1}(K) admits the precompact neighbourhood U:=∪i=1nU𝒪iU:=\cup_{i=1}^{n}U_{\mathscr{O}_{i}}, and therefore it is compact. ∎

We are now ready to conclude:

Proof of Theorem 6.7.

Let ℱ⇉F\mathcal{F}\rightrightarrows F be the typical fibre of π:𝒢→N\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to N. Consider a function f:F→[0,∞)f:F\to[0,\infty) as in Proposition 6.10.

We will construct now all the ingredients needed in Lemma 6.9.

Consider a locally finite groupoid trivialising cover for π:𝒢→N\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to N made of precompact subsets {(Vα,ψα)}α∈Λ\{(V^{\alpha},\psi^{\alpha})\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda}, and {Uα}α∈Λ\{U^{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in\Lambda} a second open cover, with U¯α⊂Vα\overline{U}^{\alpha}\subset V^{\alpha}.

The sets SαS^{\alpha} will be constructed of the form:

Sα:=f−1​({n​(i,α)|i∈ℕ}),S^{\alpha}:=f^{-1}\big(\{n(i,\alpha)\,|\,i\in\mathbb{N}\}\big),

where i↦n​(i,α)∈ℕi\mapsto n(i,\alpha)\in\mathbb{N} is an increasing sequence of integers. This ensures that each SαS^{\alpha} is ℱ\mathcal{F}-invariant. Moreover, for any x∈Fx\in F, there is i∈ℕi\in\mathbb{N} such that f​(x)<n​(i,α)f(x)<n(i,\alpha). Then the connected component of xx in F\SαF\backslash S^{\alpha} is included in f−1​([0,n​(i,α)])f^{-1}([0,n(i,\alpha)]), and hence, by properness of ff, it is precompact.

To ensure the last condition in Lemma 6.9, we construct n​(i,α)n(i,\alpha) so that the sets

Zi,α:=(ψ0α)−1​(U¯α×f−1​(n​(i,α)))⊂π0−1​(U¯α)Z^{i,\alpha}:=(\psi^{\alpha}_{0})^{-1}\big(\overline{U}^{\alpha}\times f^{-1}(n(i,\alpha))\big)\subset\pi_{0}^{-1}(\overline{U}^{\alpha})

are pairwise disjoint, i.e., for (i,α)≠(j,β)(i,\alpha)\neq(j,\beta),

(7) Zi,α∩Zj,β=∅.Z^{i,\alpha}\cap Z^{j,\beta}=\emptyset.

Since Zα=⋃i∈ℕZi,αZ^{\alpha}=\bigcup_{i\in\mathbb{N}}Z^{i,\alpha}, the required condition follows: Zα∩Zβ=∅Z^{\alpha}\cap Z^{\beta}=\emptyset, for α≠β\alpha\neq\beta.

Without loss of generality, we can assume that Λ=ℕ\Lambda=\mathbb{N} or Λ={0,…,N}\Lambda=\{0,\ldots,N\}. We consider the lexicographic ordering on ℕ×Λ\mathbb{N}\times\Lambda: (j,β)≤(i,α)(j,\beta)\leq(i,\alpha) if and only if j<ij<i, or j=ij=i and β≤α\beta\leq\alpha. We use induction with respect to this well-ordering to construct the map n:ℕ×Λ→ℕn\colon\mathbb{N}\times\Lambda\to\mathbb{N}. Set n​(0,0)=0n(0,0)=0. Suppose that nn is defined on all (j,β)(j,\beta), with (j,β)<(i,α)(j,\beta)<(i,\alpha). Define n​(i,α)n(i,\alpha) so that

n​(i,α)>sup{f∘pr2∘ψ0α​(x)|x∈π0−1​(U¯α)∩Zj,β,(j,β)<(i,α)}.n(i,\alpha)>\sup\big\{f\circ\mathrm{pr}_{2}\circ\psi_{0}^{\alpha}(x)\,|\,x\in\pi_{0}^{-1}(\overline{U}^{\alpha})\cap Z^{j,\beta},\ (j,\beta)<(i,\alpha)\big\}.

We note that the right-hand side is indeed bounded. Indeed, {U¯γ}γ∈Λ\{\overline{U}^{\gamma}\}_{\gamma\in\Lambda} is a locally finite collection of compact sets, so only finitely many β∈Λ\beta\in\Lambda satisfy U¯α∩U¯β≠∅\overline{U}^{\alpha}\cap\overline{U}^{\beta}\neq\emptyset, and so only finitely many pairs (j,β)(j,\beta) with (j,β)<(i,α)(j,\beta)<(i,\alpha) satisfy π0−1​(U¯α)∩Zj,β≠∅\pi_{0}^{-1}(\overline{U}^{\alpha})\cap Z^{j,\beta}\neq\emptyset. Hence, the supremum is taken over a compact set.

The choice of n​(i,α)n(i,\alpha) ensures (7). Indeed, if y∈Zi,α∩Zj,βy\in Z^{i,\alpha}\cap Z^{j,\beta}, we obtain

f∘pr2∘ψ0α​(y)=n​(i,α)>sup​{f∘pr2∘ψ0α​(x)|x∈π0−1​(U¯α)∩Zj,β}≥f∘pr2∘ψ0α​(y),f\circ\mathrm{pr}_{2}\circ\psi_{0}^{\alpha}(y)=n(i,\alpha)>\mathrm{sup}\{f\circ\mathrm{pr}_{2}\circ\psi_{0}^{\alpha}(x)\,|\,x\in\pi_{0}^{-1}(\overline{U}^{\alpha})\cap Z^{j,\beta}\}\geq f\circ\mathrm{pr}_{2}\circ\psi_{0}^{\alpha}(y),

which is a contradiction.

Theorem 6.7 follows now by applying Lemma 6.9. ∎

It would be interesting to understand whether source-properness is indeed a necessary condition for the existence of complete multiplicative connections.

Question 6.12.

Does every locally trivial family of Lie groupoids admit a complete multiplicative Ehresmann connection?

Appendix A Lie groupoid fibrations

Lie groupoid fibrations (recall Definition 2.1) were studied before; for the non-smooth setting see [2, 4], and for the smooth setting see [24, 10]. In this appendix, we provide simpler criteria for a functor to be a Lie groupoid fibration. We apply these to show that conclude that uniform Lie groupoid fibrations are indeed Lie groupoid fibrations.

Theorem A.1.

Let π\pi be a Lie groupoid morphism π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} with base map π0:M→N\pi_{0}\colon M\to N, and denote

π!:=π×𝒔:𝒢→ℋ×π0sM.\pi^{!}:=\pi\times\boldsymbol{s}\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H}{}_{s}\times_{\pi_{0}}M.

Any of the following conditions is equivalent to π\pi being a Lie groupoid fibration.

  1. (1)

    The maps π0\pi_{0} and π!\pi^{!} are surjective submersions.

  2. (2)

    The map π\pi is a surjective submersion and the map π!\pi^{!} is surjective.

  3. (3)

    The map π\pi is a surjective submersion, and it restricts to a surjective map between source-fibres, i.e., π:𝒔−1​(x)→𝒔−1​(π0​(x))\pi\colon\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(x)\to\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(\pi_{0}(x)) is surjective for any x∈Mx\in M.

In the proof, we will use some auxiliary results.

Lemma A.2.

Suppose f:X→Yf\colon X\to Y is a surjective submersion. Then for any smooth map g:Z→Yg\colon Z\to Y, the base change map f^:Z×fgX→Z\widehat{f}\colon Z{}_{g}\times_{f}X\to Z is a surjective submersion.

Proof.

Let z∈Zz\in Z, and let y:=g​(z)y:=g(z). Since ff is surjective, there exists x∈Xx\in X such that f​(x)=yf(x)=y. In other words (z,x)∈Z×fgX(z,x)\in Z{}_{g}\times_{f}X and f^​(z,x)=z\widehat{f}(z,x)=z. So f^\widehat{f} is surjective. Moreover, since ff is a submersion, for any x∈f−1​(y)x\in f^{-1}(y) we find a smooth local section σ:U→X\sigma:U\to X of ff, i.e., f∘σ=idUf\circ\sigma=\mathrm{id}_{U}, such that σ​(y)=x\sigma(y)=x. Then the map

σ^:g−1(U)→Z×fgX,w↦(w,σ(g(w)))\widehat{\sigma}\colon g^{-1}(U)\to Z{}_{g}\times_{f}X,\qquad w\mapsto(w,\sigma(g(w)))

is a smooth local section of f^\widehat{f} with σ^​(z)=(z,x)\widehat{\sigma}(z)=(z,x). Hence f^\widehat{f} is a submersion. ∎

Lemma A.3.

Consider a commutative diagram of vector spaces

Suppose that FF is surjective and the restriction G:ker⁡F→ker⁡fG\colon\ker F\to\ker f is surjective, then G×F:U→X×gfYG\times F\colon U\to X{}_{f}\times_{g}Y is surjective.

Proof.

Take some (x,y)∈X×gfY(x,y)\in X{}_{f}\times_{g}Y and notice that by surjectivity of FF we can find u1∈Uu_{1}\in U such that F​(u1)=yF(u_{1})=y. It follows that

f​(x−G​(u1))=f​(x)−f​(G​(u1))=f​(x)−g​(F​(u1))=f​(x)−g​(y)=0.f(x-G(u_{1}))=f(x)-f(G(u_{1}))=f(x)-g(F(u_{1}))=f(x)-g(y)=0.

Therefore, x−G​(u1)∈ker⁡fx-G(u_{1})\in\ker f, and so there exists u2∈ker⁡Fu_{2}\in\ker F for which G​(u2)=x−b​(u1)G(u_{2})=x-b(u_{1}). We define u=u1+u2u=u_{1}+u_{2} and remark that

F​(u1+u2)=F​(u1)=y,G​(u1+u2)=x⟹G×F​(u)=(x,y).F(u_{1}+u_{2})=F(u_{1})=y,\quad G(u_{1}+u_{2})=x\implies G\times F(u)=(x,y).

Therefore, G×FG\times F is surjective. ∎

Lemma A.4.

Consider a commutative diagram of sets

Then the following are equivalent:

  1. (1)

    The map G×F:U→X×gfYG\times F\colon U\to X{}_{f}\times_{g}Y is surjective.

  2. (2)

    For all z∈Zz\in Z and all x∈f−1​(z)x\in f^{-1}(z), the map F:G−1​(x)→g−1​(z)F\colon G^{-1}(x)\to g^{-1}(z) is surjective.

Proof.

(1)⟹\implies(2): Suppose G×F:U→X×gfYG\times F\colon U\to X{}_{f}\times_{g}Y is surjective and pick some arbitrary z∈Zz\in Z and x∈f−1​(z)x\in f^{-1}(z). For y∈g−1​(z)y\in g^{-1}(z) we have (x,y)∈X×gfY(x,y)\in X{}_{f}\times_{g}Y such that there exists some u∈Uu\in U with G×F​(u)=(x,y)G\times F(u)=(x,y), per assumption. This implies that F​(u)=y∈g−1​(z)F(u)=y\in g^{-1}(z) and G​(u)=xG(u)=x such that u∈G−1​(x)u\in G^{-1}(x). Therefore, the restriction F:G−1​(x)→g−1​(z)F\colon G^{-1}(x)\to g^{-1}(z) is surjective.

(2)⟹\implies(1): Suppose that for all choices z∈Zz\in Z and x∈f−1​(z)x\in f^{-1}(z) the map F:G−1​(x)→g−1​(z)F\colon G^{-1}(x)\to g^{-1}(z) is surjective. Fix some (x,y)∈X×gfY(x,y)\in X{}_{f}\times_{g}Y. This implies that x∈f−1​(g​(y))x\in f^{-1}(g(y)) and as F:G−1​(x)→g−1​(g​(y))F\colon G^{-1}(x)\to g^{-1}(g(y)) is surjective, we find some u∈G−1​(x)u\in G^{-1}(x) with F​(u)=yF(u)=y. Therefore, G×F​(u)=(x,y)G\times F(u)=(x,y) ∎

Proof of A.1.(1).

Condition (1) is part of the definition of a Lie groupoid fibration. To show that the condition is sufficient, we need to check that it implies that π\pi is a surjective submersion. Applying Lemma A.2 to f=π0:M→Nf=\pi_{0}\colon M\to N and g=𝒔:ℋ→Ng=\boldsymbol{s}\colon\mathcal{H}\to N, it follows that the induced map π^0:ℋ×π0𝒔M→ℋ\widehat{\pi}_{0}\colon\mathcal{H}{}_{\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{\pi_{0}}M\to\mathcal{H} is a surjective submersion. The decomposition π=π^0∘π!\pi=\widehat{\pi}_{0}\circ\pi^{!} shows that π\pi is a surjective submersion. ∎

Proof of A.1.(2).

As condition (2) is part of the definition of fibration, we only need to show that it implies that π!\pi^{!} is a submersion. Let g∈𝒢g\in\mathcal{G} and denote (h,x):=π!​(g)=(π​(g),𝒔​(g))(h,x):=\pi^{!}(g)=(\pi(g),\boldsymbol{s}(g)). By assumption, Tg​π:Tg​𝒢→Th​ℋT_{g}\pi:T_{g}\mathcal{G}\to T_{h}\mathcal{H} is surjective. On the other hand, by Proposition B.1 (or [21]*Corollary C.3), we have that ker⁡T​π⇉ker⁡T​π0\ker T\pi\rightrightarrows\ker T\pi_{0} is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid. In particular, Tg​𝒔:ker⁡Tg​π→ker⁡Tx​π0T_{g}\boldsymbol{s}\colon\ker T_{g}\pi\to\ker T_{x}\pi_{0} is surjective. Therefore, we conclude by Lemma A.3 that Tg​π!T_{g}\pi^{!} is surjective. So π!\pi^{!} is a submersion. ∎

Proof of A.1.(3).

Lemma A.4 shows that π!\pi^{!} is surjective exactly when π:𝒔−1​(x)→𝒔−1​(π0​(x))\pi:\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(x)\to\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(\pi_{0}(x)) is surjective for all x∈Mx\in M. So items (b) and (c) are equivalent. ∎

Example 5.5 shows that Lie groupoid fibrations indeed define a proper subclass of surjective submersions of Lie groupoids.

Remark.

We remark that in the set-theoretical setting, the source-fibrewise surjectivity of the map is used to define Lie groupoid fibrations, and this property is also called a star-surjective groupoid morphism (see, e.g., [2] and [4]).  △\vartriangle

With all the tools in place, it is easy to obtain that Lie groupoid fibrations are closed under taking pullbacks—much like surjective submersions or covering spaces.

Corollary A.5.

Let π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} be a Lie groupoid fibration and ϕ:𝒦→ℋ\phi\colon\mathcal{K}\to\mathcal{H} be a Lie groupoid morphism. Then π^:𝒦×πϕ𝒢→𝒦\widehat{\pi}:\mathcal{K}{}_{\phi}\times_{\pi}\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{K} is also a Lie groupoid fibration.

Proof.

Lemma A.2 shows that π^\widehat{\pi} is a surjective submersion. By Theorem A.1 (c), we need only show that π^\widehat{\pi} is surjective between source fibres. Denote the bases of the groupoid 𝒢\mathcal{G}, ℋ\mathcal{H} and 𝒦\mathcal{K} by MM, NN and KK, respectively. Pick some (x,y)∈K×π0ϕ0M(x,y)\in K{}_{\phi_{0}}\times_{\pi_{0}}M and k∈𝒔−1​(x)⊂𝒦k\in\boldsymbol{s}^{-1}(x)\subset\mathcal{K}. Note that

𝒔​(ϕ​(k))=ϕ0​(𝒔​(k))=ϕ0​(x)=π0​(y).\boldsymbol{s}(\phi(k))=\phi_{0}(\boldsymbol{s}(k))=\phi_{0}(x)=\pi_{0}(y).

So, (ϕ(k),y)∈ℋ×π0𝒔M(\phi(k),y)\in\mathcal{H}{}_{\boldsymbol{s}}\times_{\pi_{0}}M. As π\pi is a Lie groupoid fibration, there exists some g∈𝒢g\in\mathcal{G} such that π!​(g)=(π​(g),𝒔​(g))=(ϕ​(k),y)\pi^{!}(g)=(\pi(g),\boldsymbol{s}(g))=(\phi(k),y). So (k,g)∈𝒦×πϕ𝒢(k,g)\in\mathcal{K}{}_{\phi}\times_{\pi}\mathcal{G}, π^​(k,g)=k\widehat{\pi}(k,g)=k and 𝒔​((k,g))=(x,y)\boldsymbol{s}((k,g))=(x,y). This shows that π^\widehat{\pi} is surjective between source-fibres. ∎

Next, we show that the name uniform Lie groupoid fibration is indeed justified.

Corollary A.6.

Any uniform Lie groupoid fibration is a Lie groupoid fibration.

Proof.

Suppose that π:𝒢→ℋ\pi\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} is a uniform Lie groupoid fibration. Note that the map π∗:𝒢→π0∗​ℋ\pi^{*}\colon\mathcal{G}\to\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H} is a Lie groupoid extension, and thus, in particular, a Lie groupoid fibration. Additionally, as π0×π0:M×M→N×N\pi_{0}\times\pi_{0}\colon M\times M\to N\times N is a Lie groupoid fibration, by Corollary A.5, so is the base-change π0×π0^:π0∗​ℋ→ℋ\widehat{\pi_{0}\times\pi_{0}}\colon\pi_{0}^{*}\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}. This implies that π=π0×π0^∘π∗\pi=\widehat{\pi_{0}\times\pi_{0}}\circ\pi^{*} is also a Lie groupoid fibration. ∎

Next, we show that uniform fibrations are indeed a proper subclass of fibrations.

Example A.7.

Let ℋ⇉N\mathcal{H}\rightrightarrows N be a Lie groupoid, PP a manifold of dimP>0\dim P>0, and denote their product groupoid as 𝒢=ℋ×P⇉N×P\mathcal{G}=\mathcal{H}\times P\rightrightarrows N\times P. The projection map pr1:𝒢→ℋ\mathrm{pr}_{1}\colon\mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{H} defines a Lie groupoid fibration; it is the pullback of the trivial fibration P→{∗}P\to\{*\} along the trivial groupoid map ℋ→{∗}\mathcal{H}\to\{*\}. However, it is not uniform. Indeed

π∗:𝒢=ℋ×P→π0∗(ℋ)=ℋ×P×P,(h,p)↦(h,p,p)\pi^{*}\colon\mathcal{G}=\mathcal{H}\times{P}\to\pi_{0}^{*}(\mathcal{H})=\mathcal{H}\times{P}\times P,\quad(h,p)\mapsto(h,p,p)

is neither surjective nor a submersion.  △\vartriangle

Appendix B 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoids

The following statement characterizes 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoids as subgroupoids that are vector bundles, and has been used several times in the paper. The result generalizes the first part of [18]*Lemma 3.5, and is proven similarly.

Proposition B.1.

Let (Γ,E/⁣/𝒢,M)(\Gamma,E\mathbin{\mkern-2.0mu/\mkern-6.0mu/\mkern-2.0mu}\mathcal{G},M) be a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid. A vector subbundle S⊂ΓS\subset\Gamma over 𝒢\mathcal{G} which is a subgroupoid is a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-subgroupoid of Γ\Gamma with base S|M∩ES|_{M}\cap E.

Remark.

In the statement, we allow the vector bundle S|M∩ES|_{M}\cap E to have possibly different ranks over different connected components of MM. In fact, from the proof one can easily see that the rank of S|M∩ES|_{M}\cap E will be the same over two connected components M1M_{1} and M2M_{2} that are related by an arrow g∈𝒢g\in\mathcal{G} with 𝒔​(g)∈M1\boldsymbol{s}(g)\in M_{1} and 𝒕​(g)∈M2\boldsymbol{t}(g)\in M_{2}.  △\vartriangle

In the following auxiliary result, we also allow V1∩V2V_{1}\cap V_{2} to have different ranks over different connected components.

Lemma B.2.

Let VV be a vector bundle and V1,V2⊂VV_{1},V_{2}\subset V vector subbundles, all over the connected manifold MM. Then V1∩V2V_{1}\cap V_{2} is a vector subbundle if and only if each v∈V1∩V2v\in V_{1}\cap V_{2} can be extended to a smooth local section of VV with values in V1∩V2V_{1}\cap V_{2}.

Proof.

As V1∩V2V_{1}\cap V_{2} is the kernel of the vector bundle map V1→V/V2V_{1}\to V/V_{2}, it is a vector subbundle if and only if it has locally constant rank. As a kernel of a vector bundle map, its rank can only drop locally. The property that any v∈V1∩V2v\in V_{1}\cap V_{2} can be extended to a local section implies that the rank can only grow locally, and thus, it implies that V1∩V2V_{1}\cap V_{2} is a vector subbundle. The converse is obvious. ∎

Proof of Proposition B.1.

We divide the proof into several steps.

Step 1: S|M∩ES|_{M}\cap E is a vector subbundle of EE.

Here we identify EE with the unit section E≡𝒖​(E)⊂ΓE\equiv\boldsymbol{u}(E)\subset\Gamma, which is a vector subbundle over the unit section of 𝒢\mathcal{G}, which we also identify as M≡𝒖​(M)⊂𝒢M\equiv\boldsymbol{u}(M)\subset\mathcal{G}. So S|MS|_{M} and EE are vector subbundles of Γ|M\Gamma|_{M}. We check the criterion in Lemma B.2 for each connected component of MM. Let e∈(S∩E)xe\in(S\cap E)_{x}. Pick a local section of S|MS|_{M}, σ:U⊂M→S|M\sigma\colon U\subset M\to S|_{M}, such that σ​(x)=e\sigma(x)=e. Then 𝒔~∘σ:U→E\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}}\circ\sigma\colon U\to E is a smooth section of EE with values in S|M∩ES|_{M}\cap E such that 𝒔~∘σ​(x)=𝒔~​(e)=e\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}}\circ\sigma(x)=\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}}(e)=e.

Step 2: S|M∩CS|_{M}\cap C is a vector bundle, where C:=ker⁡s~|MC:=\ker\widetilde{s}|_{M} is the core of Γ\Gamma.

Notice that Γ|M=C⊕E\Gamma|_{M}=C\oplus E, with projection to EE given by 𝒔~:Γ|M→E\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}}\colon\Gamma|_{M}\to E and inclusion of EE given by 𝒖~:E→Γ|M\widetilde{\boldsymbol{u}}\colon E\to\Gamma|_{M}. Since SS is a groupoid, it is invariant under these two maps, which shows that S|M=(S|M∩C)⊕(S|M∩E)S|_{M}=(S|_{M}\cap C)\oplus(S|_{M}\cap E). Using Step 1, it follows that S|M∩CS|_{M}\cap C has locally constant rank, which implies the claim.

Step 3: S∩ker⁡𝐬~S\cap\ker\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}} is a vector subbundle.

We first note that ker⁡𝒔~\ker\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}} is canonically isomorphic to 𝒕∗​C\boldsymbol{t}^{*}C via the morphism:

j:𝒕∗​C→ker⁡𝒔~,(c,g)↦c⋅0g,c∈C𝒕​(g);j\colon\boldsymbol{t}^{*}C\to\ker\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}},\qquad(c,g)\mapsto c\cdot 0_{g},\quad c\in C_{\boldsymbol{t}(g)};

in other words, right translation by 0g∈Γg0_{g}\in\Gamma_{g}, denoted as r0g:C𝒕​(g)→ker⁡𝒔~gr_{0_{g}}\colon C_{\boldsymbol{t}(g)}\to\ker\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}}_{g}, is a linear isomorphism. Now as SS is a subgroupoid of Γ\Gamma, this map restricts to an isomorphism r0g:S𝒕​(g)∩C𝒕​(g)→Sg∩ker⁡𝒔~gr_{0_{g}}\colon S_{\boldsymbol{t}(g)}\cap C_{\boldsymbol{t}(g)}\to S_{g}\cap\ker\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}}_{g}. So S∩ker⁡𝒔~S\cap\ker\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}} has locally constant rank. By Lemma B.2, it follows that it is a vector subbundle.

Step 4: 𝐬~:S→S|M∩E\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}}\colon S\to S|_{M}\cap E is a surjective submersion.

First, the previous steps imply that the map is fibrewise surjective:

rank​(S|M∩E)=rank​(S|M)−rank​(S|M∩C)=rank​(S)−rank​(S∩ker⁡𝒔~)=rank​(𝒔~|S).\mathrm{rank}(S|_{M}\cap E)=\mathrm{rank}(S|_{M})-\mathrm{rank}(S|_{M}\cap C)=\mathrm{rank}(S)-\mathrm{rank}(S\cap\ker\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}})=\mathrm{rank}(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{s}}|_{S}).

Since the map covers the surjective submersion 𝒔\boldsymbol{s}, it is a surjective submersion as well.

Step 5: Conclusion.

We can conclude that S⇉S|M∩ES\rightrightarrows S|_{M}\cap E is a Lie groupoid. Being contained in Γ⇉E\Gamma\rightrightarrows E, it is compatible with the vector bundle structure, so as to be a 𝒱​ℬ\mathcal{V}\mathcal{B}-groupoid. ∎

References

  • [1] H. Amiri, H. Glöckner, and A. Schmeding (2020) Lie groupoids of mappings taking values in a Lie groupoid.. Arch. Math. (Brno) 56 (5), pp. 307–356. External Links: Document, ISSN 0044-8753 Cited by: §3.1.
  • [2] R. Brown (1970) Fibrations of groupoids. J. Algebra 15, pp. 103–132. External Links: Document, ISSN 0021-8693 Cited by: Appendix A, Remark.
  • [3] H. Bursztyn, A. Cabrera, and M. del Hoyo (2016) Vector bundles over Lie groupoids and algebroids. Adv. Math. 290, pp. 163–207. External Links: Document, ISSN 0001-8708,1090-2082, Link, MathReview (Iakovos Androulidakis) Cited by: §2.3, §3.2.1.
  • [4] B. Chen, C. Du, and Y. Wang (2020) On fibrations of Lie groupoids. J. Geom. Phys. 152, pp. 27. External Links: Document, ISSN 0393-0440 Cited by: Appendix A, Remark.
  • [5] M. Crainic, R. L. Fernandes, and I. Mărcuț (2021) Lectures on Poisson geometry. Grad. Stud. Math., Vol. 217, Providence, RI: American Mathematical Society (AMS). External Links: Document, ISBN 978-1-4704-6667-1; 978-1-4704-6666-4, ISSN 1065-7339 Cited by: §5.

Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.

BETA
